topics.title: Introduction to Optimization and Engineering Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET524 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Optimization and Engineering Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Optimization? ŌĆö The Formal Academic Definition

**Optimization** is the mathematical discipline concerned with finding the *best possible* (maximum or minimum) value of an objective function from a defined set of feasible alternatives, subject to a set of constraints imposed on the variables.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education), an optimization problem is formally stated as:

> *Given a system described by a mathematical model, find the values of the decision variables that maximize or minimize the performance criterion while satisfying all physical, economic, and operational constraints.*

Mathematically, the general structure is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \\
\text{to optimize } & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (also called criterion, cost, or merit function)
- $\mathbf{x}$ is the **vector of design (decision) variables** ŌĆö typically of dimension $n$
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ are the **inequality constraints**
- $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ are the **equality constraints**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every optimization problem in this course consists of **three essential ingredients** ŌĆö (1) at least one *decision variable* you can control, (2) an *objective function* that measures performance, and (3) a *feasible region* defined by constraints. If any one is missing, it is NOT a well-posed optimization problem.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Optimization in Plain English

Imagine you are a **Kerala housewife managing a monthly household budget of Ōé╣10,000** for groceries.

- You have to decide *how much* rice, vegetables, milk, and snacks to buy.
- You want to **minimize** the cost (objective), or **maximize** the nutrition/calories.
- But you are bound by constraints: family of 4 needs at least 2,500 kcal/day, you cannot spend more than Ōé╣10,000, and you can only buy from the local ration shop.

You don't randomly guess ŌĆö you apply logic: *substitute expensive items with cheaper equivalents, avoid waste, prioritize nutrients per rupee*. That disciplined search for the "best" allocation is **optimization**.

Now replace the housewife with an **engineer designing a steel truss bridge**:

- The *decision variables* are the cross-sectional areas of the truss members.
- The *objective* is to **minimize the total weight (cost)** of the steel.
- The *constraints* are maximum allowable stress, deflection limits, buckling resistance, and budget.

The mathematical framework is identical ŌĆö only the *context* changes. This is why optimization is called the **"engine of engineering decision-making"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Almost every engineering decision ŌĆö from the aerodynamic shape of a Boeing 737 wing, the routing of a mobile network signal, the discharge rate of a lithium-ion battery, to the layout of a CPU chip ŌĆö is the result of solving an optimization problem, either explicitly or implicitly.

---

## 1.3 Why Optimization Matters in Engineering

Engineering is fundamentally about making **trade-offs** under scarcity ŌĆö scarce material, scarce time, scarce money, scarce energy. Optimization provides the *systematic, mathematical, and reproducible* way to make these trade-offs.

The three pillars of engineering design that optimization addresses:

| Engineering Pillar | Optimization's Role |
|---|---|
| **Performance** | Maximize efficiency, speed, output, payload, profit |
| **Cost** | Minimize material, energy, time, manpower, wastage |
| **Safety/Reliability** | Enforce constraints to ensure the design does not fail |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick KTU Recall:** In any 3-mark or 14-mark question on "engineering applications of optimization," always frame your answer using the trio: **Minimize cost / Maximize performance / Subject to safety & operational constraints**. This is the examiner's expected structure.

---

## 1.4 Origin and Historical Context

- The roots of optimization trace back to the **calculus of variations** (Euler, Lagrange, 18th century).
- **Linear Programming (LP)** was formalized by **George Dantzig** in 1947 via the *Simplex Method*.
- **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** blossomed in the 1950s with **KuhnŌĆōTucker conditions (1951)**.
- **Modern metaheuristics** ŌĆö Genetic Algorithms (Holland, 1975), Simulated Annealing (Kirkpatrick, 1983), Particle Swarm (Kennedy & Eberhart, 1995) ŌĆö emerged for problems with no closed-form solution.

This historical arc matters because KTU questions often test whether you know *which method* is appropriate for *which class* of problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an Optimization Problem ŌĆö A Structured Breakdown

An optimization model is a mathematical abstraction of a real decision problem. Let us deconstruct it into its six logical building blocks.

### Block 1: Design Variables (Decision Variables)
These are the **knobs you turn** to improve the system. They are the unknown quantities the optimizer must determine.

- **Notation:** $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ or in vector form $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- **Types:**
  - *Continuous* (e.g., diameter of a pipe in mm)
  - *Discrete* (e.g., number of bolts ŌĆö must be integer)
  - *Mixed* (a combination of both)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Identify the design variables in this problem."* The trick is: any quantity that the *designer can freely choose* is a design variable. Fixed quantities (gravity, ambient temperature) are **parameters**, not variables.

### Block 2: Objective Function
A single scalar function that quantifies the *goodness* of a design.

- If we want to **minimize** cost, weight, time, or error ŌåÆ $f(\mathbf{x})$
- If we want to **maximize** profit, efficiency, output ŌåÆ we convert to minimization by negating: $\min\{-f(\mathbf{x})\}$

A problem can be a **single-objective** (most common in KTU) or **multi-objective** (Pareto-optimal solutions).

### Block 3: Constraints
Constraints represent **real-world limitations** ŌĆö physical laws, economic budgets, safety regulations, manufacturing tolerances.

Two families exist:
- **Inequality constraints** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ or $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$
- **Equality constraints** $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$

A constraint that is *active* (satisfied as equality) at the optimum is called a **binding constraint**.

### Block 4: Feasible Region (Search Space)
The set of *all* points $\mathbf{x}$ that satisfy every constraint simultaneously.

$$
S = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}
$$

The optimum **must** lie inside $S$. If $S = \emptyset$ (empty), the problem is **infeasible**.

### Block 5: Optimum Solution
- **Global Optimum** ŌĆö the *absolute best* over the entire feasible region.
- **Local Optimum** ŌĆö the best in a *neighborhood* but not globally. Many algorithms (e.g., gradient descent) may get trapped here.

### Block 6: Problem Parameters
Constants supplied by the user (material density, load magnitude, fluid viscosity). They are *not* optimized ŌĆö they are inputs.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Optimization Problems

This classification is the **single most tested topic** in KTU Module 1.

### 2.2.1 Based on the Nature of the Objective Function and Constraints

| Class | Objective | Constraints | Example Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | Linear in $\mathbf{x}$ | All linear | Simplex, Graphical |
| **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** | Nonlinear OR constraints nonlinear | At least one nonlinear | KKT, Gradient, Penalty |
| **Quadratic Programming (QP)** | Quadratic | Linear | Wolfe, HildrethŌĆōd'Alessio |
| **Geometric Programming (GP)** | Posynomial | Posynomial | Dual methods |
| **Integer Programming (IP)** | Linear | Linear, $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Branch & Bound |
| **Dynamic Programming (DP)** | Additive/Recursive | Stage-wise | Bellman's Principle |

### 2.2.2 Based on the Nature of Variables

- **Static Optimization** ŌĆö variables do not vary with time (most KTU problems).
- **Dynamic Optimization** ŌĆö variables evolve in time; governed by differential equations (Calculus of Variations, Pontryagin's Maximum Principle).

### 2.2.3 Based on the Number of Objectives

- **Single-Objective** ŌĆö one $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Multi-Objective** ŌĆö multiple conflicting objectives. The solution is a **Pareto front**, not a single point.

### 2.2.4 Based on the Nature of Equations

- **Deterministic** ŌĆö all parameters known exactly.
- **Stochastic** ŌĆö parameters are random variables (uses probability theory).

### 2.2.5 Based on the Search Space

- **Continuous-domain** optimization.
- **Discrete / Combinatorial** optimization (e.g., Travelling Salesman).

---

## 2.3 Engineering Applications ŌĆö Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 1 mandates the student to be able to *identify* optimization in real engineering contexts. The table below covers the **high-yield applications** you should be able to write about in 14-mark answers.

| Engineering Domain | Optimization Application | Typical Objective | Typical Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structural / Civil** | Truss, beam, frame design | Minimize weight / cost | Stress, deflection, buckling |
| **Mechanical Design** | Gear train, spring, shaft | Minimize volume / mass | Fatigue, stiffness, geometry |
| **Thermal Engineering** | Heat exchanger, IC engine | Maximize heat transfer / efficiency | Pressure drop, size |
| **Electrical Networks** | Power flow, filter design | Minimize power loss | Voltage, current limits |
| **Manufacturing** | Process parameter tuning | Minimize cycle time / cost | Quality tolerances |
| **Aerospace** | Airfoil, trajectory | Minimize drag / fuel | Lift, structural limits |
| **Chemical** | Reactor design, blending | Maximize yield / profit | Mass balance, equilibrium |
| **Communication** | Network routing | Minimize delay / cost | Bandwidth, QoS |
| **Machine Learning** | Model training | Minimize loss function | Regularization |
| **Economics & Finance** | Portfolio design | Maximize return | Risk budget |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the structure of this table. KTU valuation keys are *very* generous when you reproduce the canonical mathematical form correctly.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Statement | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General Optimization Problem** | $\min f(\mathbf{x}) \; s.t. \; g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Canonical KTU form |
| 2 | **Feasible Region** | $S = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j \leq 0, \; h_k = 0\}$ | All constraints satisfied |
| 3 | **Unconstrained local minimum** | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}, \; \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*) \succ 0$ | 1st-order necessary, 2nd-order sufficient |
| 4 | **Lagrangian Function** | $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_k h_k(\mathbf{x})$ | Used in KKT conditions |
| 5 | **KKT Stationarity** | $\nabla f + \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j + \sum \lambda_k \nabla h_k = \mathbf{0}$ | Generalizes Lagrange multipliers |
| 6 | **Complementary Slackness** | $\mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \; \mu_j \geq 0$ | Distinguishes active/inactive |
| 7 | **Hessian positive definiteness** | $\mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{H} \mathbf{v} > 0 \; \forall \mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | For local min (unconstrained) |
| 8 | **Number of design variables** | $n = \dim(\mathbf{x})$ | Determines dimensionality |
| 9 | **Active set size** | $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ = number of binding constraints | $\leq n$ for non-degenerate optimum |
| 10 | **Linear objective** | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{x} + c_0$ | Used in LP problems |

> [!TIP]
> **Pitfall Avoidance:** When writing inequality constraints in the standard form for KKT conditions, ALWAYS put them as $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ (i.e., "less than or equal to zero"). Mixing $\leq$ and $\geq$ is a guaranteed 1ŌĆō2 mark deduction in KTU papers.

---

## 2.5 A Simple Worked-Out Example (To Anchor the Theory)

**Problem:** A rectangular box has a volume of $32 \; \text{m}^3$ and an open top. Find the dimensions that minimize the surface area.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Design Variables.**
Let the length, width, and height be $x_1, x_2, x_3$ respectively.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Formulate the Objective Function.**
The surface area (open top, so no top panel):

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 x_2 + 2 x_1 x_3 + 2 x_2 x_3
$$

We want to **minimize** $f$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify the Constraint.**
Volume must equal 32 m┬│:

$$
h(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 x_2 x_3 - 32 = 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine into the Standard Form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 x_2 + 2 x_1 x_3 + 2 x_2 x_3 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & h(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 x_2 x_3 - 32 = 0 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **nonlinear, equality-constrained, continuous optimization problem**. (Solving it via Lagrange multipliers will be covered in Module 2, but you can already identify the structure.)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unconstrained Local Minimum ŌĆö 1st-Order Necessary Condition

We will derive the most fundamental result in optimization: at a local minimum of a *differentiable* function, the gradient must vanish.

**Setup:** Let $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuously differentiable ($C^1$). Let $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ be a local minimizer.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Taylor Series Expansion Around $\mathbf{x}^*$.**
For a small perturbation $\Delta \mathbf{x}$, we expand $f$ to first order:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta \mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x} + o(\|\Delta \mathbf{x}\|)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Local Minimum Definition.**
Since $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local minimum, for all small $\Delta \mathbf{x}$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \geq f(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

Subtracting $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ from both sides:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^\top \Delta \mathbf{x} + o(\|\Delta \mathbf{x}\|) \geq 0
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Examine Both Positive and Negative Perturbations.**
Choose $\Delta \mathbf{x} = \epsilon \mathbf{e}_i$ (a small perturbation along the $i$-th axis). Then:

$$
\epsilon \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}(\mathbf{x}^*) + o(\epsilon) \geq 0
$$

Taking $\epsilon \to 0^+$ (positive side) gives $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \geq 0$.
Taking $\epsilon \to 0^-$ (negative side) gives $\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \leq 0$.

The only way both inequalities hold is:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0
$$

Since this holds for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$, we conclude:

$$
\boxed{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}}
$$

This is the **first-order necessary condition** (FONC) for an unconstrained local minimum.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: 2nd-Order Sufficient Condition via Hessian

The FONC is *necessary* but not *sufficient* (a saddle point also has zero gradient). We need a second-order test.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Taylor Expansion to Second Order.**

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta \mathbf{x}) = f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f^\top \Delta \mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \Delta \mathbf{x} + o(\|\Delta \mathbf{x}\|^2)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Stationarity Condition $\nabla f = 0$.**
The first-order term drops:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta \mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{H} \Delta \mathbf{x} + o(\|\Delta \mathbf{x}\|^2)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Require the Quadratic Form to be Positive.**
For $\mathbf{x}^*$ to be a strict local minimum, the difference must be $> 0$ for all $\Delta \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\boxed{\Delta \mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \Delta \mathbf{x} > 0 \quad \forall \Delta \mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}}
$$

This is the definition of **positive definiteness** of the Hessian matrix.

**Practical Tests for Positive Definiteness (KTU often asks this):**
1. **Sylvester's Criterion:** All leading principal minors of $\mathbf{H}$ are strictly positive.
2. **Eigenvalue Test:** All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{H}$ are strictly positive.
3. **LDLßĄĆ Decomposition:** The $D$ matrix has strictly positive diagonal entries.

If $\mathbf{H}$ is positive *semi*-definite (some eigenvalues = 0), the test is inconclusive.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Decision Procedure for a KTU Problem

When you encounter an optimization problem in a KTU exam, apply this **4-step decision matrix**:

| Step | Question to Ask | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Identify variables** | What is the *designer* free to choose? | Vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **2. Identify objective** | What is being minimized or maximized? | $f(\mathbf{x})$ |
| **3. Identify constraints** | What physical/economic/regulatory limits exist? | $g_j \leq 0, h_k = 0$ |
| **4. Classify the problem** | Linear? Nonlinear? Convex? Discrete? | Selects the right solution method |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Most 14-mark questions on this module expect you to take a *word problem* and convert it into this standard mathematical form. The marks are awarded as: (1) correct identification of variables (2 marks), (2) correct objective function (4 marks), (3) correct constraints (4 marks), (4) classification & discussion (4 marks).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Full Formulation of a Two-Bar Truss Problem

**Problem Statement:** A two-bar truss supports a vertical load $P = 10 \; \text{kN}$ at the apex. The bars have cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$, made of steel with density $\rho = 7850 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ and yield stress $\sigma_y = 250 \; \text{MPa}$. The truss is to be designed for minimum weight.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Design Variables.**
The bars are at angles $\theta$ (with the horizontal). If the span between supports is fixed, then the *free* design variables are $A_1$ and $A_2$. So:

$$
\mathbf{x} = (A_1, A_2)^\top
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Objective Function (Weight to Minimize).**
The total volume times density:

$$
W = \rho L (A_1 + A_2)
$$

where $L$ is the bar length. To minimize $W$ is equivalent to minimizing $A_1 + A_2$ (since $\rho, L$ are constant). So:

$$
f(A_1, A_2) = A_1 + A_2
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Constraints.**

By static equilibrium, the force in each bar is:

$$
F_1 = \frac{P}{2 \sin \theta}, \quad F_2 = \frac{P}{2 \sin \theta}
$$

The stress in each bar must not exceed yield:

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{F_i}{A_i} \leq \sigma_y
$$

This gives the inequality constraints:

$$
g_i(A_i) = \frac{F_i}{A_i} - \sigma_y \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2
$$

Additionally, areas must be non-negative:

$$
A_1 \geq 0, \quad A_2 \geq 0
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Standard Form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{A_1, A_2} \quad & f = A_1 + A_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & g_1 = \frac{P}{2 \sin \theta \cdot A_1} - \sigma_y \leq 0 \\
& g_2 = \frac{P}{2 \sin \theta \cdot A_2} - \sigma_y \leq 0 \\
& A_1, A_2 > 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Classification.**
This is a **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** problem because the constraints $g_1, g_2$ contain $1/A_i$ ŌĆö a nonlinear (reciprocal) term. The objective is linear, but the constraints' nonlinearity is enough to make it NLP.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Solve (Optional, to Confirm).**
From the active constraint: $A_i = \frac{P}{2 \sigma_y \sin \theta}$. Both are equal, so:

$$
A_1^* = A_2^* = \frac{P}{2 \sigma_y \sin \theta} = \frac{10 \times 10^3}{2 \times 250 \times 10^6 \times \sin \theta}
$$

For $\theta = 60┬░$, $\sin \theta = 0.866$:

$$
A_1^* = A_2^* = \frac{10{,}000}{500 \times 10^6 \times 0.866} = 2.31 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2 = 23.1 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

---

## 3.5 Convexity ŌĆö The "Holy Grail" Property

A function $f$ is **convex** on a set $S$ if for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in S$ and $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$
f(\lambda \mathbf{x} + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{y}) \leq \lambda f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{y})
$$

**Why it matters:**
- For a convex objective and convex feasible set, **every local minimum is a global minimum**.
- This guarantees that algorithms like gradient descent will *not* get stuck in poor local optima.
- LP, QP (with positive semidefinite Hessian), and Geometric Programs are convex.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In a 14-mark problem asking "Will the optimum you find be global?", the answer is: *"Yes, IF the problem is convex. Verify convexity by checking that the Hessian $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x})$ is positive semidefinite for all $\mathbf{x}$ in the feasible region."*

---

## 3.6 Performance Measures for Optimization Algorithms

A KTU question may ask: *"How do we know if a numerical method has converged?"* Here is the standard stopping criteria:

| Criterion | Mathematical Form | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Function tolerance** | $\vert f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) - f(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert < \epsilon_1$ | Objective plateau |
| **Variable tolerance** | $\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k\| < \epsilon_2$ | Variables stable |
| **Gradient norm** | $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\| < \epsilon_3$ | Stationarity |
| **Constraint violation** | $\max_j \vert g_j(\mathbf{x}_k) \vert < \epsilon_4$ | Feasibility |
| **Maximum iterations** | $k \geq k_{\max}$ | Safety limit |

Typical values: $\epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2 = \epsilon_3 = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Pillars of an Optimization Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem] --> B[1 Design Variables x]
    A --> C[2 Objective Function f of x]
    A --> D[3 Constraints g and h]
    B --> E[Feasible Region S]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Search Space]
    F --> G[Apply Algorithm]
    G --> H[Optimal Solution x star]
    H --> I{Verify}
    I -- Yes --> J[Converged]
    I -- No --> G
    H --> K[Post-Analysis]
    K --> L[Sensitivity Analysis]
    K --> M[Check Convexity]
    K --> N[Validate in Real World]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The optimization problem is the *root node*, branching into its three essential ingredients.
- Design variables and constraints jointly define the **feasible region**.
- The algorithm searches this region, returning the optimal point.
- The verification loop ensures convergence; post-analysis checks robustness.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Optimization Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Optimization Problem] --> Q1{Number of variables}
    Q1 -- Single --> R1[Single-Variable]
    Q1 -- Multiple --> R2[Multi-Variable]
    P --> Q2{Nature of f and constraints}
    Q2 -- All linear --> S1[Linear Programming LP]
    Q2 -- Nonlinear --> S2[Nonlinear Programming NLP]
    S2 --> S3[Unconstrained]
    S2 --> S4[Equality Constrained]
    S2 --> S5[Inequality Constrained]
    Q2 -- Quadratic f, linear constraints --> S6[Quadratic Programming QP]
    P --> Q3{Variable type}
    Q3 -- Continuous --> T1[Continuous Optimization]
    Q3 -- Integer --> T2[Integer Programming]
    Q3 -- Mixed --> T3[Mixed Integer]
    P --> Q4{Determinism}
    Q4 -- Known params --> U1[Deterministic]
    Q4 -- Random params --> U2[Stochastic Optimization]
```

---

## 4.3 Engineering Application Mapping (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOM1[Structural Engineering]
        S1[Truss Design] --> S2[Minimize Weight]
        S3[Beam Sizing] --> S2
        S4[Frame Layout] --> S2
    end
    subgraph DOM2[Thermal Systems]
        T1[Heat Exchanger] --> T2[Maximize Heat Transfer]
        T3[Insulation] --> T4[Minimize Heat Loss]
    end
    subgraph DOM3[Electrical]
        E1[Circuit Design] --> E2[Minimize Power Loss]
        E3[Filter Tuning] --> E4[Maximize SNR]
    end
    subgraph DOM4[Manufacturing]
        M1[Process Param] --> M2[Minimize Cycle Time]
        M3[Quality Control] --> M4[Minimize Defects]
    end
    subgraph DOM5[Aerospace]
        A1[Airfoil Shape] --> A2[Minimize Drag]
        A3[Trajectory] --> A4[Minimize Fuel]
    end
    CORE[Common Optimization Engine] --> DOM1
    CORE --> DOM2
    CORE --> DOM3
    CORE --> DOM4
    CORE --> DOM5
    CORE --> O1[Objective Function]
    CORE --> O2[Constraints]
    CORE --> O3[Algorithm]
```

**Interpretation:** Regardless of the domain ŌĆö civil, mechanical, electrical, manufacturing, or aerospace ŌĆö the *core* optimization engine remains the same. Only the *inputs* (objective, constraints) change. This is the unifying beauty of optimization theory.

---

## 4.4 The Optimization Solution Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Real World Problem] --> B[Step 2: Mathematical Formulation]
    B --> C[Step 3: Classification LP NLP QP etc]
    C --> D[Step 4: Select Algorithm]
    D --> E[Step 5: Code or Manual Solution]
    E --> F[Step 6: Verify Feasibility]
    F --> G{Converged}
    G -- No --> H[Adjust Algorithm or Initial Guess]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes --> I[Step 7: Post Optimality Analysis]
    I --> J[Step 8: Sensitivity Check]
    J --> K[Step 9: Engineering Validation]
    K --> L[Final Design]
```

---

## 4.5 Global vs. Local Optimum (Conceptual Picture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Search Space] --> B[Local Minimum A]
    A --> C[Local Minimum B]
    A --> D[Global Minimum]
    B -.-> E[Gradient zero but not globally best]
    C -.-> F[Gradient zero but not globally best]
    D ==> G[Lowest value in entire domain]
    style D fill:gold
    style G fill:lightyellow
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE (End Semester Examination), Part A carries **5 questions of 3 marks each**. Below are two model questions with answer keys.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**"Define optimization. List the essential components of an optimization problem with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Optimization is the mathematical process of selecting the best element from a set of available alternatives with respect to some criterion.

The three essential components are:

1. **Design Variables** ŌĆö the parameters the designer can control. *Example:* In a cylindrical pressure vessel, the radius $r$ and height $h$.
2. **Objective Function** ŌĆö a scalar measure of performance to be minimized or maximized. *Example:* Material cost = $c_1 \pi r^2 h + c_2 (2 \pi r h)$ for side and end surfaces.
3. **Constraints** ŌĆö restrictions on the variables. *Example:* Volume $\pi r^2 h = V_0$ (a fixed capacity requirement).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for components with example)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]
**"Differentiate between local and global optimum. Why is convexity important?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Local Optimum** $\mathbf{x}^*$: A point such that $f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for all $\mathbf{x}$ in a small neighborhood $N(\mathbf{x}^*)$ of $\mathbf{x}^*$. *(1 mark)*
- **Global Optimum** $\mathbf{x}^{**}$: A point such that $f(\mathbf{x}^{**}) \leq f(\mathbf{x})$ for **all** $\mathbf{x}$ in the entire feasible region. *(1 mark)*
- **Importance of Convexity**: For a convex objective function over a convex feasible set, *every* local minimum is automatically a *global* minimum. This eliminates the risk of an algorithm returning a sub-optimal solution and guarantees robust convergence. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and provide an internal choice between two alternatives. The pattern is: **Question A OR Question B**. Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**A manufacturing firm produces two products P and Q. The profit per unit is Ōé╣50 for P and Ōé╣40 for Q. The firm has 240 hours of machine time and 100 hours of labour available. Product P requires 4 hours of machine time and 1 hour of labour. Product Q requires 2 hours of machine time and 2 hours of labour. Formulate this as a Linear Programming Problem and identify all components.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Formulate the LPP (7 marks)

**Step 1: Decision Variables.**
Let $x_1$ = number of units of product P produced, $x_2$ = number of units of product Q produced.

**Step 2: Objective Function.**
The profit is to be maximized:

$$
\max Z = 50 x_1 + 40 x_2
$$

**Step 3: Constraints.**

- Machine time: $4 x_1 + 2 x_2 \leq 240$ (hours available)
- Labour time: $x_1 + 2 x_2 \leq 100$ (hours available)
- Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Step 4: Standard LPP Form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & Z = 50 x_1 + 40 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 4 x_1 + 2 x_2 \leq 240 \\
& x_1 + 2 x_2 \leq 100 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*(4 marks for objective + constraints; 2 marks for variable identification; 1 mark for non-negativity)*

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Identify Components and Classify (7 marks)

**Identification Table:**

| Component | Description | In This Problem |
|---|---|---|
| Design Variables | $x_1, x_2$ | Units of P, Q to produce |
| Parameters | Profit/unit, hours/unit | 50, 40, 4, 2, 1, 2 |
| Objective Function | $Z = 50x_1 + 40x_2$ | Maximize profit |
| Constraints | Machine & labour | Two inequalities |
| Feasible Region | All $(x_1, x_2)$ satisfying constraints | Polygon in 1st quadrant |

**Classification:**
- Linear Programming Problem (objective and constraints are all linear).
- 2 variables, 2 constraints ŌåÆ can be solved by **Graphical Method**.
- Continuous variables (not integer).
- Deterministic (parameters known).

**Solving Graphically (for completeness):**
The constraints intersect at:
- Machine line: $4x_1 + 2x_2 = 240$ ŌåÆ passes through $(60, 0)$ and $(0, 120)$.
- Labour line: $x_1 + 2x_2 = 100$ ŌåÆ passes through $(100, 0)$ and $(0, 50)$.

Corner points of the feasible region: $(0,0)$, $(60, 0)$, $(0, 50)$, and the intersection of the two lines.

Solving the two constraints simultaneously:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 x_1 + 2 x_2 &= 240 \\
x_1 + 2 x_2 &= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting: $3 x_1 = 140 \Rightarrow x_1 = 46.67$, $x_2 = 26.67$.

Profit: $Z = 50(46.67) + 40(26.67) = 2333.33 + 1066.67 = 3400$.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 \approx 47, x_2 \approx 27, Z_{\max} = Ōé╣3,400$.

*(3 marks for component table, 2 marks for classification, 2 marks for graphical interpretation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Adapted]**

**A company wants to design a cylindrical can to hold 500 ml of juice. The cost of the material for the side and bottom is Ōé╣0.02/cm┬▓, but the top costs Ōé╣0.03/cm┬▓ (because of the pull-tab mechanism). Formulate the problem to find the dimensions that minimize cost. State the assumptions made.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Mathematical Formulation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Decision Variables.**
Let $r$ = radius of the cylinder (cm), $h$ = height of the cylinder (cm).

**Step 2: Objective Function.**

Surface areas:
- Bottom (circular): $A_b = \pi r^2$
- Top (circular): $A_t = \pi r^2$
- Side (curved): $A_s = 2 \pi r h$

Total cost:

$$
C(r, h) = 0.02 \cdot \pi r^2 + 0.03 \cdot \pi r^2 + 0.02 \cdot 2 \pi r h
$$

$$
C(r, h) = 0.05 \pi r^2 + 0.04 \pi r h
$$

**Step 3: Constraint (Volume Fixed).**
500 ml = 500 cm┬│:

$$
\pi r^2 h = 500 \implies h = \frac{500}{\pi r^2}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Reduce.**
Substitute $h$ into $C$:

$$
C(r) = 0.05 \pi r^2 + 0.04 \pi r \cdot \frac{500}{\pi r^2} = 0.05 \pi r^2 + \frac{20}{r}
$$

**Step 5: Unconstrained 1D Problem.**

$$
\min_{r > 0} \; C(r) = 0.05 \pi r^2 + \frac{20}{r}
$$

**Step 6: Solve via Calculus.**
$\frac{dC}{dr} = 0.10 \pi r - \frac{20}{r^2} = 0$

$$
0.10 \pi r^3 = 20 \implies r^3 = \frac{20}{0.10 \pi} = \frac{200}{\pi} \approx 63.66
$$

$$
r^* \approx 3.99 \approx 4 \; \text{cm}
$$

Then: $h^* = \frac{500}{\pi (4)^2} = \frac{500}{16 \pi} \approx 9.95 \approx 10 \; \text{cm}$.

**Step 7: Minimum Cost.**

$$
C_{\min} = 0.05 \pi (4)^2 + \frac{20}{4} = 0.80 \pi + 5 \approx 2.51 + 5 = Ōé╣7.51
$$

*(3 marks formulation, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks calculus solution)*

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Assumptions and Discussion (7 marks)

**Assumptions Made:**
1. The can is a *perfect* right circular cylinder (no taper, no seam loss).
2. The material thickness is negligible compared to dimensions (thin-wall assumption).
3. The can holds *exactly* 500 ml with no headspace.
4. The cost is strictly proportional to surface area (linear cost model).
5. No waste factor in material cutting.

**Engineering Insights:**
- The optimal shape has $h \approx 2.5 \, r$, which closely matches real-world juice cans (taller than they are wide).
- If the top and bottom had the same cost, the optimal would be $h = 2r$ (a classic result).
- Sensitivity: A 10% increase in the top cost would shift the optimum toward slightly shorter, wider cans.

**Classification:**
- Single-variable, continuous, unconstrained (after substitution) optimization.
- Solvable by classical calculus ($\frac{dC}{dr} = 0$).
- Convex problem: $\frac{d^2 C}{dr^2} = 0.10 \pi + \frac{40}{r^3} > 0$, so the critical point is a guaranteed global minimum.

*(3 marks assumptions, 2 marks insights, 2 marks classification + verification)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in Module 1 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Non-Negativity Constraints** ŌĆö Always state $x_i \geq 0$ explicitly. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Confusing "Maximize" with "Minimize" Conventions** ŌĆö KTU standard form is **minimization**. If your problem is maximization, convert it via $\max f = -\min(-f)$.
> 3. **Mixing $\leq$ and $\geq$ Sign Conventions** ŌĆö Standard KKT conditions require $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$. Inconsistent signs will cost you 1ŌĆō2 marks in Module 2/3 questions.
> 4. **Calling Fixed Quantities "Design Variables"** ŌĆö Gravity, given loads, material properties are *parameters*, not variables.
> 5. **Not Justifying the Classification** ŌĆö If you say it's an NLP, *briefly* state why (nonlinear function/constraint). Saying just "it is nonlinear" is not enough.
> 6. **Skipping the Non-Negativity in LP Formulation** ŌĆö Every LPP must end with $x_1, x_2, \dots \geq 0$.
> 7. **Forgetting to State Assumptions** ŌĆö In a design problem, list at least 2ŌĆō3 assumptions (thin wall, perfect geometry, linear cost, etc.).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam!):**

- [x] **Optimization** = finding the *best* (min or max) value of an objective function over a feasible region.
- [x] Three pillars: **Decision Variables + Objective + Constraints**.
- [x] **Design Variable** = what the designer can choose. **Parameter** = a fixed input.
- [x] **Feasible Region** = all $\mathbf{x}$ that satisfy every constraint.
- [x] **Global vs. Local Optimum**: global is best overall; local is best in a neighborhood.
- [x] **Convex problem** ŌćÆ local min = global min ŌćÆ algorithms are reliable.
- [x] **FONC (1st-order necessary condition)** for unconstrained min: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
- [x] **SOSC (2nd-order sufficient condition)**: Hessian $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ positive definite.
- [x] **Sylvester's Criterion**: All leading principal minors of $\mathbf{H}$ positive ŌćÆ $\mathbf{H} \succ 0$.
- [x] **LP**: linear objective + linear constraints ŌåÆ Simplex / Graphical method.
- [x] **NLP**: at least one nonlinearity ŌåÆ KKT / Penalty / Gradient methods.
- [x] **Active constraint** = binding at optimum (satisfied as equality).
- [x] **KKT conditions** (preview): stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness.
- [x] **Standard form for KKT**: $\min f, \; g_j \leq 0, \; h_k = 0, \; x_i \geq 0$.
- [x] **Sensitivity Analysis** = checking how the optimum changes with parameter variations.
- [x] **Stopping criteria**: function tolerance, variable tolerance, gradient norm, iteration limit.
- [x] **Engineering applications span**: structural, thermal, electrical, manufacturing, aerospace, chemical, ML.
- [x] **History**: Dantzig (LP, 1947), KuhnŌĆōTucker (1951), Holland (GA, 1975).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Question Patterns for This Topic:**
> - 3-mark: Definition of optimization, list components, classify a given problem.
> - 14-mark: Take a word problem, formulate it (variables, objective, constraints), classify it, and discuss solution methodology.
> - Common traps: forgetting non-negativity, mixing constraint signs, misidentifying parameters as variables.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Formulation of an Optimization Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET524 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Formulation of an Optimization Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Formulation of an Optimization Problem** is the systematic process of translating a real-world decision-making scenario into a precise mathematical model consisting of an **objective function**, a set of **decision (design) variables**, and a set of **constraints** (equality and/or inequality), so that the optimal value of the objective function can be determined subject to the constraints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an optimization problem in its canonical form is expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } \mathbf{x} &= [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T \in \mathbb{R}^n \\
\text{that minimizes (or maximizes) } & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ is the **vector of decision variables** ($n$ variables).
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **scalar objective function** to be optimized.
- $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ are the **inequality constraints** ($m$ in number).
- $h_k(\mathbf{x})$ are the **equality constraints** ($p$ in number).
- $x_i^{(L)}$ and $x_i^{(U)}$ are the **lower and upper bounds** (side constraints) on each variable.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö Planning the Cheapest Biryani Order for a College Fest**

Imagine you are the student coordinator of a college fest and you must decide **how many plates of biryani (Ōé╣120/plate) and how many juice boxes (Ōé╣40/box)** to order to feed **at least 200 students** within a **budget of Ōé╣20,000**, and the kitchen can prepare a **maximum of 150 plates of biryani per hour**.

Here, you instinctively perform an **optimization**:
- **Decision Variables:** Number of biryani plates ($x_1$), number of juice boxes ($x_2$).
- **Objective Function:** Minimize total cost ŌåÆ $f(x_1, x_2) = 120x_1 + 40x_2$.
- **Constraints:**
  - Feed at least 200 students ŌåÆ $x_1 + x_2 \ge 200$ (an inequality constraint).
  - Budget limit ŌåÆ $120x_1 + 40x_2 \le 20{,}000$ (another inequality constraint).
  - Kitchen capacity ŌåÆ $x_1 \le 150$ (a side/upper-bound constraint).
  - Cannot order negative quantities ŌåÆ $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$ (non-negativity constraints).

The process of **converting this real-life problem into equations and inequalities** is precisely the *mathematical formulation* of an optimization problem. You didn't need a calculator yet ŌĆö you just translated the story into math. The next modules teach you how to **solve** it.

---

### 1.3 Why Mathematical Formulation is the Critical First Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 places heavy emphasis on the *formulation stage* because **more than 60% of real-world engineering problems fail to reach a good solution ŌĆö not because of bad algorithms ŌĆö but because of poor mathematical formulation.**

Formulation acts as the **bridge** between:
1. The **physical / managerial / engineering reality**, and
2. The **algorithmic solution methods** (covered in Modules 2ŌĆō5).

A good formulation must be:
- **Faithful** ŌĆö it must capture every essential feature of the original problem.
- **Compact** ŌĆö it should use the minimum number of variables and constraints.
- **Solvable** ŌĆö it must lie in the domain where known algorithms converge.
- **Sensitive** ŌĆö it must allow engineers to interpret the effect of parameter changes.

---

### 1.4 Terminology Snapshot (Used Throughout the Module)

| Term | Standard Symbol | Quick Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Decision (Design) Variable | $x_i$ | Quantity the engineer controls |
| Objective Function | $f(\mathbf{x})$ | The single quantity to optimize |
| Constraint | $g_j, h_k$ | Restrictions imposed by nature or design |
| Feasible Region / Feasible Set | $S$ | Set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying all constraints |
| Feasible Solution | $\mathbf{x} \in S$ | A point that obeys every constraint |
| Optimal Solution | $\mathbf{x}^*$ | A feasible point giving the best objective value |
| Global Optimum | $\mathbf{x}^{**}$ | Best feasible point over the entire domain |
| Local Optimum | $\mathbf{x}^{*}$ | Best feasible point in a neighbourhood only |
| Active Constraint | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Constraint binding at the optimum |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of an Optimization Problem

Every well-formulated optimization problem ŌĆö whether it comes from mechanical design, electrical networks, structural engineering, or operations research ŌĆö has exactly **four logical pillars**. The KTU examiner expects students to be able to *identify* and *write* these four components for any given word problem.

#### **Pillar 1 ŌĆö The Decision Variables**

- These are the **quantities we are free to choose**.
- They are *unknowns* in the mathematical sense.
- They are typically denoted $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$, or collectively as a column vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Classification of decision variables:**

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous** | Can take any real value within a range | Thickness of a beam (cm) |
| **Discrete / Integer** | Take only whole-number values | Number of machines to install |
| **Binary (0ŌĆō1)** | Take only the values 0 or 1 | Build a plant: yes (1) or no (0) |
| **Mixed** | A combination of the above | Number of workers (integer) + hours of operation (continuous) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In KTU Module 1, we restrict ourselves to **continuous variables**. Integer and binary variables are introduced later in Integer Programming (Module 4/5 of the syllabus).

---

#### **Pillar 2 ŌĆö The Objective Function**

- A **single scalar function** $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ that quantifies the *quality* of any choice of decision variables.
- It is the **"what are we optimising"** element.
- Two canonical forms:
  - **Minimization:** $\min f(\mathbf{x})$
  - **Maximization:** $\max f(\mathbf{x})$
- **Single objective** vs. **Multi-objective**:
  - Single objective: optimize one criterion (e.g., minimize cost).
  - Multi-objective: optimize several criteria (e.g., minimize weight *and* maximize strength). These are typically converted to a single objective via the **weighted sum method**:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 f_1(\mathbf{x}) + w_2 f_2(\mathbf{x}) + \dots + w_k f_k(\mathbf{x})
$$

where $w_i \ge 0$ and $\sum w_i = 1$ are the *preference weights* assigned by the designer.

---

#### **Pillar 3 ŌĆö The Constraints**

Constraints represent the **real-world restrictions** that the decision variables must obey. There are three principal categories:

1. **Equality Constraints** ŌĆö must be satisfied exactly:

$$
h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p
$$

*Examples:* Force balance equations, conservation of mass, energy, momentum.

2. **Inequality Constraints** ŌĆö bound the region of feasibility:

$$
g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m
$$

*Examples:* Stress $\le$ allowable stress, temperature $\le$ melting point, cost $\le$ budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** All inequality constraints are always written in the form "$g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$". If the problem states "$\ge$" or "$\ge 0$" for a quantity, multiply by $-1$ to bring it to the standard form.

3. **Side Constraints / Bounds** ŌĆö explicit limits on individual variables:

$$
x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

*Examples:* Length must be positive, voltage must be between 0 V and 240 V.

---

#### **Pillar 4 ŌĆö The Feasible Region**

- The **set of all points** $\mathbf{x}$ that satisfy every constraint simultaneously.
- Mathematically: $S = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}\}$.
- The optimization problem essentially asks: *Within $S$, find the $\mathbf{x}$ that yields the best $f(\mathbf{x})$.*
- If $S = \emptyset$ (empty set), the problem is **infeasible** ŌĆö no solution exists.
- If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is unbounded over $S$, the problem is **unbounded**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table ŌĆö it is the backbone of every KTU Module 1 question.**

| # | Component | Mathematical Form | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decision vector | $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 2 | Objective (min form) | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ | $f : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ |
| 3 | Objective (max form) | $\max f(\mathbf{x})$ | Convert via $f' = -f$ |
| 4 | Inequality constraint | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | $j = 1, \dots, m$ |
| 5 | Equality constraint | $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | $k = 1, \dots, p$ |
| 6 | Lower bound | $x_i \ge x_i^{(L)}$ | Side constraint |
| 7 | Upper bound | $x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$ | Side constraint |
| 8 | Non-negativity | $x_i \ge 0$ | $x_i^{(L)} = 0$ |
| 9 | Feasible set | $S = \{\mathbf{x} \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ | $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 10 | Optimal value | $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in S} f(\mathbf{x})$ | $\mathbf{x}^*$ optimal |
| 11 | Multi-objective scalarization | $f = \sum w_i f_i,\; w_i \ge 0,\; \sum w_i = 1$ | Weighted sum |
| 12 | Number of variables | $n$ | Decision space dim |
| 13 | Total constraints | $m + p$ | Constraint count |

---

### 2.3 Classification of Optimization Problems (KTU Module 1 Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to be able to *classify* a given problem into the correct type.** The following hierarchy is examinable.

- **Based on the nature of $f$ and $g_j, h_k$:**
  - **Linear Programming (LP):** $f$, $g_j$, $h_k$ all linear in $\mathbf{x}$.
  - **Non-Linear Programming (NLP):** At least one of $f$, $g_j$, $h_k$ is non-linear.
    - **Unconstrained NLP:** No $g_j$ or $h_k$.
    - **Constrained NLP:** At least one $g_j$ or $h_k$ present.
- **Based on the nature of variables:**
  - **Continuous Optimization:** $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$.
  - **Integer / Discrete Optimization:** $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$.
  - **Mixed-Integer:** Some continuous, some integer.
- **Based on the nature of the search space:**
  - **Static / Parametric:** Decision made once, fixed parameters.
  - **Dynamic:** Decisions evolve over time (covered in Module 5).
- **Based on the number of objectives:**
  - **Single-objective:** One $f(\mathbf{x})$.
  - **Multi-objective:** Multiple conflicting $f_i(\mathbf{x})$ ŌĆö Pareto optimality applies.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Typical Formulation |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Design** | Minimize weight of a truss subject to stress $\le$ allowable |
| **Electrical Power** | Minimize transmission losses subject to power balance |
| **Civil / Structural** | Minimize cost of concrete mix subject to strength constraint |
| **Production / OR** | Maximize profit subject to labour, material, machine hours |
| **Chemical Process** | Maximize yield subject to temperature, pressure, composition |
| **Aerospace** | Minimize drag subject to lift, structural, and fuel constraints |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The General Mathematical Derivation of the Canonical Form

We begin with a *generic* real-world problem statement and derive its canonical mathematical form step by step.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the Goal (Optimization Direction).**
The first logical question is: *Are we trying to minimize or maximize something?*
If the problem says "minimize cost" or "shortest path" or "least material", we use:

$$
\min f(\mathbf{x})
$$

If it says "maximize profit", "highest efficiency", or "longest life", we use:

$$
\max f(\mathbf{x})
$$

A standard algebraic trick is to convert any maximization into minimization:

$$
\max f(\mathbf{x}) \equiv \min \left\{-f(\mathbf{x})\right\}
$$

This is called the **dual conversion** and is heavily used in KTU Module 2 (LPP) problems.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the Decision Variables.**
List every independent quantity the designer is allowed to choose. If there are $n$ such quantities, label them $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ and form the column vector $\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express the Objective as a Function of $\mathbf{x}$.**
Using the underlying engineering or business equations, express the quantity being optimized (cost, profit, weight, time, etc.) in terms of the decision variables. This yields $f(\mathbf{x})$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Express Each Restriction Mathematically.**
Every real-world restriction is converted into either:
- An equation $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, or
- An inequality $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, or
- A side bound $x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Assemble the Canonical Form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{optimize } \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, \dots, p \\
& x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Verify Completeness.**
Check that every real-world restriction has been captured, and that no extra artificial constraint has been added.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Production Planning (Classic KTU Style)

> A factory produces two products A and B. Each unit of A gives a profit of Ōé╣50 and each unit of B gives Ōé╣60. Producing one unit of A requires 2 hours on Machine M1 and 1 hour on Machine M2. Producing one unit of B requires 1 hour on M1 and 3 hours on M2. Machine M1 is available for at most 100 hours and M2 for at most 90 hours. The market can absorb at most 40 units of A and at most 30 units of B. **Formulate the problem to maximize profit.**

**Solution ŌĆö Step 1: Identify the goal.**
The problem says "maximize profit". Decision variables are non-negative real numbers (assume continuous production). So:

$$
\text{optimize (in the sense of max)} \quad f(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 2: Identify the decision variables.**
Let:
- $x_1$ = number of units of product A produced.
- $x_2$ = number of units of product B produced.

So:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^2
$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 3: Write the objective function.**
Profit = (profit per A) ├Ś (units of A) + (profit per B) ├Ś (units of B)

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = 50 x_1 + 60 x_2
$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 4: Write each constraint.**

*Machine M1 hours:* 2 hours per A, 1 hour per B, total available 100 hours.

$$
2x_1 + 1x_2 \le 100
$$

*Machine M2 hours:* 1 hour per A, 3 hours per B, total available 90 hours.

$$
1x_1 + 3x_2 \le 90
$$

*Market limit on A:* At most 40 units.

$$
x_1 \le 40
$$

*Market limit on B:* At most 30 units.

$$
x_2 \le 30
$$

*Non-negativity:*

$$
x_1 \ge 0, \quad x_2 \ge 0
$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 5: Assemble the canonical formulation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & Z = 50 x_1 + 60 x_2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 2x_1 + x_2 \le 100 \\
& x_1 + 3x_2 \le 90 \\
& x_1 \le 40 \\
& x_2 \le 30 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution ŌĆö Step 6 (Optional LPP conversion to standard min form).**
For modules that require standard minimization form, multiply the objective by $-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & Z' = -50 x_1 - 60 x_2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 2x_1 + x_2 \le 100 \\
& x_1 + 3x_2 \le 90 \\
& x_1 \le 40 \\
& x_2 \le 30 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Engineering Design (Truss / Beam Optimization)

> A cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section supports a tip load $P = 10$ kN. The maximum bending stress must not exceed $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 200$ MPa, and the maximum deflection at the free end must not exceed $\delta_{\text{allow}} = 5$ mm. The beam is made of steel with Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa, length $L = 2$ m. **Formulate the optimization problem to minimize the volume (weight) of the beam.**

**Step 1: Decision variables.**
Let $b$ = width (m), $h$ = depth (m) of the rectangular cross-section. So:

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} b \\ h \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Objective function.**
Volume of the beam (length ├Ś cross-section area):

$$
V(b, h) = L \cdot b \cdot h = 2 \cdot b \cdot h
$$

**Step 3: Bending stress constraint.**
For a cantilever with end load $P$, the maximum bending moment is $M_{\max} = P L$. The maximum bending stress is:

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{6 P L}{b h^2} \le \sigma_{\text{allow}}
$$

Substituting values ($P = 10\,000$ N, $L = 2$ m, $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 200 \times 10^6$ Pa):

$$
\dfrac{6 \times 10{,}000 \times 2}{b h^2} \le 200 \times 10^6
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\dfrac{120{,}000}{b h^2} \le 200 \times 10^6
$$

Rearranging to standard $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ form:

$$
g_1(b, h) = 200 \times 10^6 \cdot b h^2 - 120{,}000 \le 0
$$

**Step 4: Deflection constraint.**
For a cantilever with end load, $\delta_{\max} = \dfrac{4 P L^3}{E b h^3}$. The constraint is:

$$
\dfrac{4 P L^3}{E b h^3} \le \delta_{\text{allow}}
$$

Substituting values ($P = 10^4$ N, $L = 2$ m, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\delta_{\text{allow}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m):

$$
\dfrac{4 \times 10^4 \times 8}{200 \times 10^9 \cdot b h^3} \le 5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
g_2(b, h) = \dfrac{4 P L^3}{\delta_{\text{allow}} \cdot E} - b h^3 \le 0
$$

**Step 5: Side constraints and non-negativity.**

$$
b \ge 0, \quad h \ge 0
$$

**Step 6: Final canonical form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & V(b, h) = 2 b h \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 200 \times 10^6 \cdot b h^2 - 1.2 \times 10^5 \le 0 \\
& \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^5}{5 \times 10^{-3}} - 200 \times 10^9 \cdot b h^3 \le 0 \\
& b, h \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **non-linear programming (NLP)** problem because the objective and the constraints contain products of variables. KTU students must be able to *classify* this correctly.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Diet / Blending Problem (KTU Classic)

> A dietician must prepare a meal using two foods F1 and F2. Each unit of F1 costs Ōé╣30 and provides 4 units of protein and 6 units of vitamins. Each unit of F2 costs Ōé╣20 and provides 3 units of protein and 1 unit of vitamin. The meal must provide at least 12 units of protein and 8 units of vitamins. **Formulate the problem to minimize cost.**

**Decision variables:**
- $x_1$ = units of F1 used.
- $x_2$ = units of F2 used.

**Objective (minimize cost):**

$$
C = 30 x_1 + 20 x_2
$$

**Constraints:**

*Protein:* $4x_1 + 3x_2 \ge 12$ ŌĆö convert to standard $\le 0$ form:

$$
g_1 = 12 - 4x_1 - 3x_2 \le 0
$$

*Vitamins:* $6x_1 + 1x_2 \ge 8$ ŌĆö convert to standard $\le 0$ form:

$$
g_2 = 8 - 6x_1 - x_2 \le 0
$$

*Non-negativity:* $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$.

**Final canonical form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & C = 30 x_1 + 20 x_2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 12 - 4x_1 - 3x_2 \le 0 \\
& 8 - 6x_1 - x_2 \le 0 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation of the Weighted-Sum Multi-Objective Reduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 commonly tests the conversion of a multi-objective problem into a single-objective form.**

Suppose we have $k$ objectives $f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \dots, f_k(\mathbf{x})$ to be optimized simultaneously. We assign non-negative weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_k$ such that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i = 1
$$

The composite scalar objective becomes:

$$
F(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot f_i(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Justification of the normalization $\sum w_i = 1$:** This ensures that no objective dominates the others purely due to scale. For instance, if $f_1$ is "weight in kg" (values around 10) and $f_2$ is "cost in Ōé╣" (values around 1000), an unweighted sum would let the cost dominate. Normalizing both objectives to a $[0, 1]$ scale before summation (or using $w_i$ as reciprocals of typical magnitudes) removes this bias.

**Note:** The weights are *subjective* ŌĆö they encode the designer's preference. KTU Module 1 problems typically specify the weights in the question itself.

---

### 3.6 Key Mathematical Properties (Required for Higher Modules)

> [!NOTE]
> The following properties are *introduced* in Module 1 but applied in Modules 2ŌĆō5. Examiners often award 1ŌĆō2 marks in 14-mark questions for stating these correctly.

- **Convexity of a set:** A set $S$ is *convex* if for any two points $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \in S$ and any $\lambda \in [0, 1]$:

$$
\lambda \mathbf{x}^{(1)} + (1 - \lambda) \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \in S
$$

- **Convexity of a function:** $f(\mathbf{x})$ is convex if:

$$
f\bigl(\lambda \mathbf{x}^{(1)} + (1 - \lambda) \mathbf{x}^{(2)}\bigr) \le \lambda f(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) + (1 - \lambda) f(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})
$$

- **Feasible region in LP is always a convex polyhedron** (or polytope if bounded).
- **A linear objective over a convex polyhedron achieves its optimum at an extreme point (vertex)** ŌĆö this is the foundation of the Simplex Method (Module 2).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Anatomy of a Formulated Optimization Problem

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the logical flow that an engineer follows while going from a real-world word problem to a fully-formulated optimization model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real World Problem Statement"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Goal minimize or maximize"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Identify Decision Variables x1, x2, ... xn"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Formulate Objective Function f of x"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Enumerate Restrictions"]
    E --> F["Equality Constraints hk of x = 0"]
    E --> G["Inequality Constraints gj of x le 0"]
    E --> H["Side Bounds xi Lower le xi le xi Upper"]
    F --> I["Step 5: Assemble Canonical Model"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Step 6: Classify LP NLP Integer Dynamic"]
    J --> K["Step 7: Verify Feasibility and Completeness"]
    K --> L["Final Mathematical Model ready for solver"]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö The Decision Variable Hierarchy

This diagram classifies the decision variables that the student must recognize while formulating an optimization problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DV["Decision Variables x"] --> C1["Continuous real valued"]
    DV --> C2["Discrete integer valued"]
    DV --> C3["Binary 0 or 1"]
    C1 --> S1["Used in LP and NLP Modules 1 to 3"]
    C2 --> S2["Used in Integer Programming Module 4"]
    C3 --> S3["Used in 0 1 Programming and Combinatorial Optimization"]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Constraint Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CON["Constraints"] --> EQ["Equality Constraints hk of x = 0"]
    CON --> INEQ["Inequality Constraints gj of x le 0"]
    CON --> SB["Side Constraints bounds on xi"]
    EQ --> EX1["Example Force balance Energy conservation"]
    INEQ --> EX2["Example Stress le allowable Cost le budget"]
    SB --> EX3["Example Voltage between 0 and 240 Length positive"]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram ŌĆö Feasible Region Visualization (Conceptual 2D)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Constraint 1 boundary"] --> R["Feasible Region S"]
    P2["Constraint 2 boundary"] --> R
    P3["Constraint 3 boundary"] --> R
    P4["Constraint 4 boundary"] --> R
    R --> OPT["Optimal Point x star"]
    OBJ["Objective function level sets"] -. "touches at x star" .-> OPT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each constraint "cuts off" a portion of the plane. The intersection of all the surviving portions is the **feasible region $S$**. The optimum is the point in $S$ where the objective function's level curve is *tangent* (or just touches) the boundary of $S$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FORM["Mathematical Formulation"] --> CHECK{"Are f, gj, hk all linear?"}
    CHECK -- "Yes" --> LP["Linear Programming LP ŌĆö Solved by Simplex"]
    CHECK -- "No" --> NLP["Non Linear Programming NLP ŌĆö Solved by KKT or numerical methods"]
```
