# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow ŌĆö Solving an Unconstrained Multivariable Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given f of x1, x2, ..., xn"] --> B["Step 1: Compute partial derivatives"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Form the gradient vector nabla f"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Solve nabla f of x equals 0"]
    D --> E{"Step 4: Stationary points found?"}
    E -- "No real solution" --> F["No stationary points exist on R^n"]
    E -- "Yes" --> G["Step 5: Compute Hessian Matrix H"]
    G --> H["Step 6: Apply Sylvester Criterion / Eigenvalue test"]
    H --> I{"Step 7: Definiteness?"}
    I -- "Positive Definite" --> J["Conclusion: Strict Local Minimum"]
    I -- "Negative Definite" --> K["Conclusion: Strict Local Maximum"]
    I -- "Positive Semi-Definite" --> L["Conclusion: Possible Minimum - needs higher order test"]
    I -- "Negative Semi-Definite" --> M["Conclusion: Possible Maximum - needs higher order test"]
    I -- "Indefinite" --> N["Conclusion: Saddle Point"]
    L --> O["END"]
    M --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    N --> O
    F --> O
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Evaluate f at candidate point x star"] --> Q["Compute nabla f at x star"]
    Q --> R{"nabla f equals zero?"}
    R -- "No" --> S["Not a stationary point - move on"]
    R -- "Yes" --> T["Stationary point candidate confirmed"]
    T --> U["Compute Hessian H of x star"]
    U --> V{"Apply Eigenvalue test"}
    V -- "All lambda i greater than 0" --> W["Block A: Positive Definite - LOCAL MINIMUM"]
    V -- "All lambda i less than 0" --> X["Block B: Negative Definite - LOCAL MAXIMUM"]
    V -- "Mixed signs" --> Y["Block C: Indefinite - SADDLE POINT"]
    V -- "Some lambda i zero" --> Z["Block D: Semi-Definite - INCONCLUSIVE - higher order test required"]
```

## 4.3 Geometric Visualisation of Stationary Point Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Bowl shaped surface - PD Hessian"] --> A1["Local Minimum: lowest point in valley"]
    B["Dome shaped surface - ND Hessian"] --> B1["Local Maximum: peak of mountain"]
    C["Saddle shaped surface - Indefinite Hessian"] --> C1["Saddle Point: mountain pass between two valleys"]
    D["Flat plateau - PSD Hessian"] --> D1["Degenerate: infinitely many optima along ridge"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the first-order necessary condition for a point $\mathbf{x}^\*$ to be a local minimum of a continuously differentiable function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Statement:** If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is continuously differentiable ($f \in C^1$) on an open set containing $\mathbf{x}^\*$, and $\mathbf{x}^\*$ is a local minimum of $f$, then:
> $$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\*) = \mathbf{0}$$
> This means all $n$ partial derivatives vanish simultaneously:
> $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\bigg|_{\mathbf{x}^\*} = 0, \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
> **(1 mark ŌĆö statement, 1 mark ŌĆö expansion, 1 mark ŌĆö interpretation as $n$ simultaneous equations)**

---

### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Hessian matrix of a function $f(\mathbf{x})$ and state Sylvester's criterion for positive definiteness.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition:** The Hessian $H(\mathbf{x})$ is the $n \times n$ symmetric matrix whose $(i,j)$-th element is the second partial derivative:
> $$H_{ij}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\partial^2 f(\mathbf{x})}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$$
> **(1.5 marks)**
> **Sylvester's Criterion:** A real symmetric matrix $H$ is positive definite if and only if **all** of its leading principal minors are strictly positive:
> $$\Delta_k > 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
> where $\Delta_k = \det(H_k)$ and $H_k$ is the top-left $k \times k$ submatrix. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**Find and classify all stationary points of the function:**
$$
f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2 + 2x_1 x_2 - 6x_1 - 4x_2 + 13
$$
**OR**
**Use Newton's method to derive the update rule $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - H^{-1} \nabla f$ for minimising a quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$ where $Q$ is positive definite.**

#### **Part (a) ŌĆö Stationary Point Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives:
$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4x_1 + 2x_2 - 6, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 + 2x_1 - 4
$$

**Step 2:** Set them to zero simultaneously:
$$
4x_1 + 2x_2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2x_1 + x_2 = 3 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$
$$
2x_1 + 2x_2 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 + x_2 = 2 \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve the linear system. Subtract (ii) from (i):
$$
(2x_1 + x_2) - (x_1 + x_2) = 3 - 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 1
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back into (ii): $1 + x_2 = 2 \Rightarrow x_2 = 1$.

**Step 5:** Stationary point: $\mathbf{x}^\* = (1, 1)$. Function value: $f(1,1) = 2 + 1 + 2 - 6 - 4 + 13 = \mathbf{8}$. **(7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for gradient, 2 marks for solving, 1 mark for point, 2 marks for value)**

#### **Part (b) ŌĆö Second-Order Classification (7 Marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute the Hessian:
$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} \\ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Sylvester's criterion:
$$
\Delta_1 = 4 > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$
$$
\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (4)(2) - (2)(2) = 8 - 4 = 4 > 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3:** Since both leading principal minors are strictly positive, $H$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 4:** Conclusion: By the Second-Order Sufficient Condition, $\mathbf{x}^\* = (1, 1)$ is a **strict local (in fact, global) minimum** of $f$. The minimum value is $f(1,1) = 8$. **(7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for Hessian, 2 marks for minors, 1 mark for definiteness, 2 marks for conclusion)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to compute only the diagonal second partials ($\partial^2 f / \partial x_1^2$ and $\partial^2 f / \partial x_2^2$) and forget the off-diagonal cross partial. If you write $H = \text{diag}(4, 2)$, you will get a **determinant of 8** instead of **4**, leading to a wrong classification. The Hessian is **always** a full symmetric matrix including cross terms.

---

### **Question B-B.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**A.** Find the stationary point of $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 + 3)^2 + 5$ and classify it using the Hessian. Verify your answer by completing the square.

**OR**

**B.** Consider the function $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^3 - 3x_1 x_2^2$. Find all its stationary points and classify each one. Discuss why the second-order test alone is insufficient in this case and outline the higher-order reasoning required.

#### **Solution Path A ŌĆö Simple Quadratic (7 + 7 Marks)**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Gradient and Stationary Point (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Expand (optional) and compute partial derivatives:
$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 2), \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 + 3)
$$

**Step 2:** Set to zero:
$$
x_1 - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 2; \quad x_2 + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = -3
$$

**Step 3:** Unique stationary point: $\mathbf{x}^\* = (2, -3)$. Value: $f(2, -3) = 0 + 0 + 5 = 5$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Hessian and Completing the Square (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Hessian is constant:
$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 2I
$$

**Step 2:** $\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$, $\Delta_2 = 4 > 0$. Hence $H$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 3:** Completing the square (geometric confirmation):
$$
f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 + 3)^2 + 5 \geq 0 + 0 + 5 = 5
$$
with equality **only** at $(2, -3)$. Hence $\mathbf{x}^\*$ is the **global minimum**.

---

#### **Solution Path B ŌĆö Degenerate Cubic (7 + 7 Marks)**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Stationary Points (7 Marks):**

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 = 3(x_1 - x_2)(x_1 + x_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -6x_1 x_2
$$

Setting both to zero: Either $x_1 = 0$ **or** $x_2 = 0$ (from the second equation).
- If $x_1 = 0$: first equation gives $-3x_2^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$. Point: $\mathbf{0}$.
- If $x_2 = 0$: first equation gives $3x_1^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$. Same point.

**Only stationary point:** $\mathbf{x}^\* = (0, 0)$ with $f(0,0) = 0$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Hessian and Insufficiency (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Hessian at origin:
$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} 6x_1 & -6x_2 \\ -6x_2 & -6x_1 \end{bmatrix}\bigg|_{(0,0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Zero Hessian is **positive semi-definite** (and also negative semi-definite) ŌĆö the test is **inconclusive**.

**Step 3:** Direct inspection: along the line $x_2 = 0$, $f(x_1, 0) = x_1^3$ ŌĆö negative for $x_1 < 0$, positive for $x_1 > 0$. Along $x_1 = 0$, $f(0, x_2) = 0$ ŌĆö a flat direction. Therefore $\mathbf{x}^\* = (0,0)$ is a **saddle point** (no extremum), but the second-order test cannot determine this. **Higher-order Taylor terms are required**, which is why the SOSC is only **sufficient** and not **necessary** in degenerate cases.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** conclude that a zero Hessian implies a minimum. Many students lose 3-4 marks by writing "since $H = 0$ and $H$ is positive semi-definite, this is a minimum." This is a classic error. The correct conclusion is "**inconclusive ŌĆö higher-order test required**" and you must then **demonstrate the test** by sampling directions as shown above.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Unconstrained problem:** $\min f(\mathbf{x}), \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with **no** equality or inequality constraints.
- [x] **FONC:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\*) = \mathbf{0}$ ŌĆö necessary for any interior local extremum.
- [x] **Gradient:** $n \times 1$ column vector of first partial derivatives.
- [x] **Hessian:** $n \times n$ symmetric matrix of second partial derivatives; used to detect curvature.
- [x] **SONC for minimum:** $H$ must be **positive semi-definite** at $\mathbf{x}^\*$.
- [x] **SOSC for strict local minimum:** $H$ must be **positive definite** at $\mathbf{x}^\*$.
- [x] **SOSC for strict local maximum:** $H$ must be **negative definite** at $\mathbf{x}^\*$.
- [x] **Saddle point:** $H$ is **indefinite** (eigenvalues of both signs).
- [x] **Degenerate case:** $H$ is semi-definite ŌĆö **second-order test is inconclusive**; sample directions or use higher-order Taylor expansion.
- [x] **Sylvester's criterion:** All leading principal minors strictly positive $\Rightarrow$ $H$ is PD.
- [x] **Eigenvalue criterion:** All eigenvalues strictly positive $\Rightarrow$ $H$ is PD.
- [x] **Convexity shortcut:** If $f$ is convex on $\mathbb{R}^n$, **every** stationary point is the **global** minimum.
- [x] **Steepest descent direction:** $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) / \Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) \Vert$.
- [x] **Newton's update rule:** $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - H^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_k) \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ ŌĆö converges in one step for pure quadratics.
- [x] **Taylor expansion (local model):** $f(\mathbf{x}^\* + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^\*) + \nabla f^T \Delta \mathbf{x} + \frac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^T H \Delta \mathbf{x}$.
- [x] **Always check:** Gradient $\rightarrow$ Hessian $\rightarrow$ Definiteness $\rightarrow$ Classification. Never skip the curvature test.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Day Tip:** When asked to "find and classify" a stationary point, present your answer in this order: **(1) Gradient components, (2) Stationary point coordinates, (3) Hessian matrix, (4) Principal minor signs, (5) Definiteness verdict, (6) Final classification with the theorem name quoted.** Examiners award stepwise method marks ŌĆö even if your final answer is wrong, you will still earn 60-70% of the marks by following this disciplined structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multivariable Optimization with Constraints (Basic Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET524 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multivariable Optimization with Constraints (Basic Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Multivariable Optimization with Constraints** is the mathematical discipline concerned with determining the optimal value (minimum or maximum) of an objective function $f(\mathbf{x}) = f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ of several decision variables, subject to a set of restrictions called **constraints** that the variables must satisfy.

A general constrained optimization problem is stated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$ is the vector of decision variables.
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**.
- $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ are **inequality constraints**.
- $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ are **equality constraints**.
- The set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying every constraint is called the **feasible region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Scope (Basic Concept Only):** In this introductory module, students are expected to understand *what* a constrained optimization problem is, the role of the Lagrangian, and the meaning of Lagrange multipliers. The complete KarushŌĆōKuhnŌĆōTucker (KKT) treatment is reserved for higher modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Mountain Hiker

Imagine a hiker who wants to reach the **highest peak** of a hill (maximize elevation $f$) but is **restricted to a marked trail** (the constraint). The hiker is free to walk *along* the trail in both directions, but cannot leave it. The "best" point is reached when walking further along the trail no longer increases the elevation ŌĆö i.e., the gradient of the hill is perpendicular to the trail's direction.

This perpendicularity condition is the **geometric essence of the Lagrange multiplier rule**: at the optimum, the gradient of the objective $\nabla f$ is parallel (in fact, anti-parallel) to the gradient of the constraint $\nabla g$.

## 1.3 Classification of Optimization Problems

| Type | Constraint Status | Tools |
|:-----|:------------------|:------|
| Unconstrained | None | $\nabla f = 0$ |
| Equality constrained | $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Lagrange Multipliers |
| Inequality constrained | $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | KKT Conditions |
| Mixed | Both types | Generalized Lagrangian |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why constraints matter in engineering:** Almost every real engineering decision is bounded. Minimizing the weight of a truss is subject to strength and stiffness constraints. Maximizing profit is subject to budget, manpower, and material constraints. Without a constraint-handling tool, classical multivariable calculus is insufficient.

## 1.4 Distinction Between Unconstrained and Constrained Optima

- In the **unconstrained** case, the stationary point is found by setting $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.
- In the **constrained** case, the stationary point must satisfy $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ **and** lie on (or inside) the feasible region defined by the constraints.
- A point that is optimal without constraints may become infeasible (or sub-optimal) once constraints are imposed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lagrangian Function (Equality Constraints)

For a single equality constraint $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, the **Lagrangian function** $\mathcal{L}$ introduces an auxiliary scalar variable $\lambda$, called the **Lagrange multiplier**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \lambda) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \lambda \, h(\mathbf{x})
$$

For $m$ equality constraints:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j \, h_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The multiplier $\lambda$ has a powerful **economic / physical interpretation**: it represents the *sensitivity* of the optimal objective value to a unit change in the right-hand side of the constraint. In design, this is the **shadow price** ŌĆö how much the optimum improves per unit relaxation of the constraint.

## 2.2 Necessary Conditions for a Constrained Extremum

For a smooth objective and smooth equality constraints, a **local extremum** $\mathbf{x}^*$ with associated multiplier $\lambda^*$ must satisfy:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^*, \lambda^*) = 0
$$

$$
h(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0
$$

Written component-wise for two variables:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} + \lambda \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_1} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} + \lambda \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_2} = 0
$$

$$
h(x_1, x_2) = 0
$$

This is a system of $n + 1$ equations in $n + 1$ unknowns.

## 2.3 Geometric Interpretation

At a constrained optimum, the gradient vectors of the objective and the constraint are collinear:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = - \lambda^* \nabla h(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

The constant $\lambda^*$ is the proportionality constant. The sign of $\lambda^*$ encodes whether the constraint is *active* in a helpful or harmful sense.

## 2.4 Extension to Inequality Constraints (KKT ŌĆö Basic Idea)

When constraints are inequalities $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, the **KarushŌĆōKuhnŌĆōTucker (KKT)** framework generalises the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x})
$$

The basic KKT conditions for a minimisation problem are:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0
$$

$$
g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0, \quad \forall i
$$

$$
\lambda_i \ge 0, \quad \forall i
$$

$$
\lambda_i \, g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad \forall i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The last condition is called **complementary slackness**. It states: either the constraint is active ($g_i = 0$) and the multiplier is non-zero ($\lambda_i \ge 0$), or the constraint is inactive ($g_i < 0$) and the multiplier must be zero ($\lambda_i = 0$). It can never be the case that both are non-zero.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Type |
|:--------------------|:--------|:-----|
| $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Objective function | Scalar |
| $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | Inequality constraint | Set of $m$ |
| $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Equality constraint | Set of $p$ |
| $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \lambda)$ | Lagrangian function | Scalar |
| $\nabla f$ | Gradient of $f$ | $n$-vector |
| $\lambda$ | Lagrange multiplier | Scalar |
| $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0$ | Stationarity condition | $n$ equations |
| $\lambda \, g = 0$ | Complementary slackness | Scalar |
| $\nabla f + \lambda \nabla h = 0$ | Equivalently $ \nabla f = -\lambda \nabla h$ | Geometric condition |
| $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}^*, \lambda^*)$ | Binding constraint equality | Scalar |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural design:** Minimise weight subject to a maximum allowable stress constraint.
- **Manufacturing:** Minimise production cost subject to quality and throughput constraints.
- **Electrical engineering:** Minimise power loss in a transmission line subject to voltage-drop limits.
- **Operations Research:** Maximise profit subject to resource and demand constraints.
- **Machine learning:** Constrained training where model weights must lie inside a regularisation ball.

> [!NOTE]
> The Lagrangian method transforms a constrained problem into an unconstrained one by absorbing constraints into the objective. This single trick underpins nearly every modern numerical optimiser used in engineering today.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö Equality Constraint (Lagrange Method)

**Problem:** Find the minimum of the function

$$
f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2
$$

subject to the linear equality constraint

$$
h(x, y) : x + y - 1 = 0
$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Form the Lagrangian

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = f(x, y) + \lambda \, h(x, y)
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + y^2 + \lambda (x + y - 1)
$$

The multiplier $\lambda$ is unrestricted in sign (equality constraint).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Set Up the Stationarity System

Take partial derivatives with respect to each of the three unknowns $(x, y, \lambda)$ and equate them to zero.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2x + \lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 2y + \lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = x + y - 1 = 0
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the System Analytically

From the first two equations:

$$
2x + \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = -2x
$$

$$
2y + \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = -2y
$$

Equating the two expressions for $\lambda$:

$$
-2x = -2y \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = y
$$

Substitute into the third equation $x + y = 1$:

$$
x + x = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2x = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{1}{2}
$$

Therefore $y = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\lambda = -2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = -1$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate the Objective at the Optimum

$$
f\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}\right) = \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{1}{4} = \tfrac{1}{2}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Geometric Verification

At the optimum $\nabla f = (1, 1)^T$ and $\nabla h = (1, 1)^T$. Indeed $\nabla f = -\lambda \nabla h$ with $\lambda = -1$, confirming collinearity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative $\lambda = -1$ means: relaxing the constraint $x + y = 1$ to $x + y = 1 + \delta$ would *decrease* the objective by $1 \cdot \delta$. In other words, the constraint is *tight* and a unit increase in the constraint bound *hurts* the objective.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Economic Interpretation of $\lambda$

**Problem:** A firm produces quantities $x$ and $y$ of two products. Profit (in Ōé╣1000) is

$$
P(x, y) = 5x + 4y
$$

Total raw material available is fixed:

$$
2x + 3y = 12
$$

Find the profit-maximising production plan and the shadow price of the material.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Lagrangian

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = 5x + 4y + \lambda (12 - 2x - 3y)
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö First-Order Conditions

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 5 - 2\lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 4 - 3\lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = 12 - 2x - 3y = 0
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve

From the first: $\lambda = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$. From the second: $\lambda = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.33$. There is an inconsistency ŌĆö the problem has no interior optimum with $x, y \ge 0$. The maximum occurs at a **boundary corner**.

Corner 1: $x = 6, y = 0 \Rightarrow P = 30$. Corner 2: $x = 0, y = 4 \Rightarrow P = 16$.

**Maximum profit = Ōé╣30,000 at $(x, y) = (6, 0)$.** This illustrates that the classical Lagrange method requires convexity assumptions; without them, the stationary point may not exist or may not be the global optimum.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Procedure Checklist

| Step | Action | Result |
|:-----|:-------|:-------|
| 1 | Identify objective $f(\mathbf{x})$ and constraints | Problem statement |
| 2 | Form Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_i h_i + \sum \mu_i g_i$ | Augmented function |
| 3 | Compute $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0$ | $n$ equations |
| 4 | Append constraint equations $h_j = 0, g_i \le 0$ | $m + p$ more equations |
| 5 | Solve the full system | Candidate point $\mathbf{x}^*, \lambda^*$ |
| 6 | Check feasibility and second-order (Hessian) conditions | Classify min / max / saddle |
| 7 | Interpret $\lambda^*$ as shadow price | Economic insight |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Two Equality Constraints

**Problem:** Find the extremum of

$$
f(x, y, z) = x^2 + y^2 + z^2
$$

subject to

$$
x + y + z = 6, \quad x + 2y + 3z = 10
$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö Lagrangian

$$
\mathcal{L} = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 + \lambda_1 (6 - x - y - z) + \lambda_2 (10 - x - 2y - 3z)
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Stationarity

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2x - \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 2y - \lambda_1 - 2\lambda_2 = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z} = 2z - \lambda_1 - 3\lambda_2 = 0
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the 5├Ś5 Linear System

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$
2y - 2x - \lambda_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_2 = 2(y - x)
$$

Subtracting the second from the third:

$$
2z - 2y - \lambda_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_2 = 2(z - y)
$$

Therefore $y - x = z - y$, i.e., $2y = x + z$, or $x, y, z$ are in arithmetic progression. Combine with the two constraints and solve. The unique solution is

$$
x = 1, \quad y = 2, \quad z = 3
$$

giving $f = 1 + 4 + 9 = 14$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart ŌĆö Constrained Optimisation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Problem] --> B[Identify Objective Function f of x]
    B --> C[Identify Equality Constraints h j of x equal 0]
    C --> D[Identify Inequality Constraints g i of x less equal 0]
    D --> E[Construct Lagrangian L = f + sum lambda j h j + sum mu i g i]
    E --> F[Compute Partial Derivatives of L wrt each x k]
    F --> G[Set Derivatives to Zero: grad x L = 0]
    G --> H[Append Constraint Equations]
    H --> I{System Solvable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Obtain Candidate x star, lambda star, mu star]
    I -- No --> K[Re-examine Problem Formulation]
    J --> L[Verify Feasibility: g i less equal 0, h j = 0]
    L --> M[Check Second Order Hessian Conditions]
    M --> N{Positive or Negative Definite?}
    N -- Positive Definite --> O[Confirmed Local Minimum]
    N -- Negative Definite --> P[Confirmed Local Maximum]
    N -- Indefinite --> Q[Saddle Point: Reject]
    O --> R[Report Optimum and Shadow Prices]
    P --> R
    K --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Geometric Interpretation of Lagrange Multiplier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlaneXY[Feasible Domain in x y plane]
        Obj["Objective Contour f = c1"]:::obj
        Con["Constraint Curve h x y = 0"]:::con
        Tangent["Tangent Point x star, y star"]:::pt
        GradF["Vector grad f"]:::gf
        GradH["Vector grad h"]:::gh
    end
    Obj --- Tangent
    Con --- Tangent
    Tangent --- GradF
    Tangent --- GradH
    GradF -. "Anti-parallel scaled by negative lambda" .- GradH

    classDef obj fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef con fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef pt  fill:#FFB4B4,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gf  fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef gh  fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#06C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram ŌĆö Components of a Constrained Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer[Problem Input]
        I1[Decision Variables x1 x2 ... xn]
        I2[Objective f of x]
        I3[Constraints g and h]
    end

    subgraph ProcessLayer[Lagrangian Engine]
        P1[Build Lagrangian L]
        P2[Differentiate wrt x and lambda]
        P3[Solve Stationarity System]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Solution Output]
        O1[Optimal x star]
        O2[Multiplier lambda star]
        O3[Shadow Price Interpretation]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P2 --> O3
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Tree ŌĆö Classification of Optima

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimum Types] --> B[Unconstrained]
    A --> C[Constrained]
    B --> B1[Local Minimum]
    B --> B2[Local Maximum]
    B --> B3[Saddle Point]
    C --> D1[Equality Only: Lagrange Method]
    C --> D2[Inequality Only: KKT Method]
    C --> D3[Mixed: Generalised Lagrangian]
    D1 --> E1[grad f plus lambda grad h = 0]
    D2 --> E2[Complementary Slackness Holds]
    D3 --> E3[All KKT Conditions Apply]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** *Define a constrained optimisation problem. Differentiate between equality and inequality constraints with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

A constrained optimisation problem seeks the optimal value (minimum or maximum) of an objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to restrictions on the decision variables. Mathematically:

$$
\text{Optimise } f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0
$$

**Equality constraint** $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ forces the variable to lie *exactly* on a surface. Example: heat transfer through a wall of fixed thickness $\Rightarrow$ thickness $= 0.1$ m.

**Inequality constraint** $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ allows the variable to lie *inside* a region. Example: maximum allowable stress $\sigma \le 250$ MPa in a steel member.

Equality constraints are handled by the Lagrange multiplier method, while inequality constraints require the KKT framework.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** *What is a Lagrange multiplier? Explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda$ is a scalar auxiliary variable introduced when converting a constrained problem into an unconstrained Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \lambda) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \lambda \, h(\mathbf{x})
$$

At the optimum, $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0$, which yields $\nabla f = -\lambda \nabla h$.

**Physical significance:** $\lambda$ measures the rate of change of the optimal objective value $f^*$ per unit change in the right-hand side of the constraint. In economics it is the **shadow price**; in mechanical design it is the **sensitivity of the optimum to the constraint**. A positive $\lambda$ means the constraint is *binding and costly*; a zero $\lambda$ means the constraint is *inactive* and can be ignored.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 1]** A manufacturing firm produces two products $x$ and $y$. The cost function (in Ōé╣100) is

$$
C(x, y) = x^2 + 2y^2 - xy
$$

The firm must fulfil a production quota of total output $x + y = 6$ units.

**(a)** Formulate the constrained minimisation problem and obtain the Lagrangian. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve for the optimal $x^*, y^*$ and the minimum cost. Interpret the value of $\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Formulation (7 marks)**

The problem is:

$$
\min_{x, y} \; C(x, y) = x^2 + 2y^2 - xy
$$

subject to

$$
h(x, y) = x + y - 6 = 0
$$

The Lagrangian is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + 2y^2 - xy + \lambda (x + y - 6)
$$

The variables are $x, y$ (decision) and $\lambda$ (auxiliary multiplier).

**(b) Solution (7 marks)**

Compute the first-order partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2x - y + \lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = 4y - x + \lambda = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = x + y - 6 = 0
$$

From the first two equations, eliminate $\lambda$:

$$
2x - y = - \lambda = x - 4y
$$

Therefore

$$
2x - y = x - 4y \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = -3y
$$

Substitute into the constraint $x + y = 6$:

$$
-3y + y = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -2y = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = -3
$$

$$
x = -3y = 9
$$

The negative $y = -3$ is *infeasible* in a production context (cannot produce a negative quantity), so the unconstrained-Lagrange critical point lies *outside* the practical domain. The constrained minimum on the *feasible* segment ($x, y \ge 0, x + y = 6$) occurs at a corner.

Endpoints of the feasible line segment:
- $x = 0, y = 6$: $C = 0 + 72 - 0 = 72$.
- $x = 6, y = 0$: $C = 36 + 0 - 0 = 36$.

**Minimum cost on the feasible domain = Ōé╣3,600 at $(x, y) = (6, 0)$.**

> **Note for examiner:** This question beautifully illustrates that the classical Lagrange method gives an interior critical point, but the *practical* optimum is at a boundary where the unconstrained method fails. Award 2 marks extra if the student identifies this corner solution.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 1]** A company wishes to allocate a marketing budget of Ōé╣50,000 between two advertising channels, Online ($x$) and Print ($y$), to maximise brand awareness $A$ measured by:

$$
A(x, y) = 30 \ln x + 20 \ln y
$$

**(a)** Form the Lagrangian for the budget constraint. Write down the KKT stationarity conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve for the optimal $x^*$ and $y^*$. Compute the optimal awareness $A^*$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Lagrangian & Stationarity (7 marks)**

The problem is:

$$
\max_{x, y} \; A(x, y) = 30 \ln x + 20 \ln y
$$

subject to

$$
g(x, y) = x + y - 50000 \le 0
$$

The Lagrangian (with multiplier $\mu \ge 0$ for the inequality) is:

$$
\mathcal{L} = 30 \ln x + 20 \ln y + \mu (50000 - x - y)
$$

KKT stationarity conditions:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = \frac{30}{x} - \mu = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y} = \frac{20}{y} - \mu = 0
$$

$$
x + y \le 50000, \quad \mu \ge 0, \quad \mu (x + y - 50000) = 0
$$

**(b) Optimal Solution (7 marks)**

From the first two conditions:

$$
\mu = \frac{30}{x} = \frac{20}{y}
$$

$$
30 y = 20 x \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac{2}{3} x
$$

Since $\mu > 0$ would hold only if the budget constraint is binding, assume $x + y = 50000$:

$$
x + \frac{2}{3} x = 50000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{5}{3} x = 50000
$$

$$
x^* = 30000, \quad y^* = 20000
$$

Then

$$
\mu = \frac{30}{30000} = 0.001
$$

Optimal awareness:

$$
A^* = 30 \ln 30000 + 20 \ln 20000
$$

$$
A^* = 30 (10.309) + 20 (9.903) = 309.27 + 198.06 = 507.33
$$

**Interpretation:** Spend Ōé╣30,000 on online and Ōé╣20,000 on print. The multiplier $\mu = 0.001$ means: an additional Ōé╣1 of budget would increase awareness by approximately $0.001$ utility units (shadow price of the budget).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the third (or higher) stationarity equation** ŌĆö students often set $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x = 0$ and $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y = 0$ but forget $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \lambda = 0$ (the original constraint). This loses **2ŌĆō3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Confusing the sign of the multiplier** ŌĆö in maximisation, $\lambda$ has the opposite sign convention to minimisation. Always check the problem type.
> 3. **Reporting a saddle point as the optimum** ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct **1ŌĆō2 marks** if the second-order Hessian check is missing.
> 4. **Ignoring feasibility** ŌĆö an algebraic solution may give $x < 0$ or violate a constraint. Always verify before declaring the optimum. KTU specifically tests this in 2023 and 2024 papers.
> 5. **Skipping the shadow-price interpretation** ŌĆö the multiplier $\lambda$ is not just a number; examiners award 1ŌĆō2 marks for *interpreting* it in the problem's physical/economic context.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **constrained optimisation problem** has an objective plus one or more constraints restricting the feasible region.
- The **feasible region** is the set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying every constraint.
- The **Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \lambda_j h_j + \sum \mu_i g_i$ converts a constrained problem into an unconstrained one.
- **Stationarity:** $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = 0$ gives a system of $n$ equations.
- **Constraint equation:** $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ or $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ adds the remaining equations / inequalities.
- **Geometric meaning:** $\nabla f$ is parallel to $\nabla h$ at the optimum, with $\lambda$ as the proportionality constant.
- **KKT conditions** (basic): stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility ($\mu \ge 0$), and complementary slackness ($\mu_i g_i = 0$).
- **Shadow price** interpretation: $\lambda$ measures the sensitivity of the optimum to a unit change in the constraint bound.
- Always verify **feasibility** and **second-order conditions** before declaring the optimum.
- For an $n$-variable problem with $p$ equality constraints, you must solve a system of $n + p$ equations in $n + p$ unknowns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Weightage Note:** Constrained optimisation basics typically carry **8ŌĆō14 marks** per question paper (Module 1). Mastery of the Lagrangian, the first-order system, and the shadow-price interpretation is essential for scoring above 80% in PEAET524.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Karush-Kuhn-Tucker Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET524 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Karush-Kuhn-Tucker Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Conditions** are a set of **first-order necessary conditions** that must be satisfied by any optimal solution of a **nonlinear programming (NLP) problem** subject to inequality and equality constraints, provided certain regularity conditions (constraint qualifications) hold at the optimum.

Mathematically, consider the general nonlinear programming problem:

$$\min f(x)$$

subject to

$$g_i(x) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_j(x) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

$$x \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

If $x^*$ is a **local optimum** and the **constraint qualification** (such as LICQ ŌĆö Linear Independence Constraint Qualification) holds at $x^*$, then there exist vectors of **Lagrange multipliers** $\lambda^* = (\lambda_1^*, \lambda_2^*, \dots, \lambda_m^*)$ and $\mu^* = (\mu_1^*, \mu_2^*, \dots, \mu_p^*)$ such that:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** KKT conditions are an extension of the classical method of Lagrange multipliers to problems involving **inequality constraints** and are essential for solving constrained optimization problems in engineering design, economics, and operations research.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **hiking on a hilly terrain** and wish to find the **highest point** (optimum) in a region bounded by a fence (constraint). At the highest reachable point:

1. **If the peak is inside the region** (unconstrained), the slope must be **flat** ŌĆö this is the classical $\nabla f = 0$ condition.
2. **If the peak is on the fence boundary** (constrained), the **gradient of the terrain** must be **balanced** by the **normal force from the fence** pushing you back. The fence only pushes; it never pulls you inward. This is precisely the **complementary slackness** condition.

Thus, KKT conditions are the **mathematical expression of force balance at an optimum** under the influence of active constraints.

### 1.3 Key Components

- **Stationarity:** Gradient of the Lagrangian vanishes.
- **Primal Feasibility:** All original constraints are satisfied.
- **Dual Feasibility:** Multipliers for inequality constraints are non-negative.
- **Complementary Slackness:** Either the constraint is active or its multiplier is zero.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used:** Multipliers are dimensionless scaling factors, with values in $\mathbb{R}$. Constraint qualification index $\lambda_i$ is denoted using the **Greek letter lambda** with $i = 1, 2, \dots, m$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of the Lagrangian Function

The KKT framework begins by augmenting the objective function with all constraints, weighted by their respective multipliers. For the problem stated above, the **Lagrangian function** is:

$$L(x, \lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j h_j(x)$$

where $\lambda_i \geq 0$ are multipliers for inequality constraints, and $\mu_j \in \mathbb{R}$ are multipliers for equality constraints (sign-unrestricted).

### 2.2 The Four KKT Conditions

Let $x^*$ be the candidate optimal point with corresponding multipliers $\lambda^*, \mu^*$. The KKT conditions are:

**ŌæĀ Stationarity Condition**

$$\nabla_x L(x^*, \lambda^*, \mu^*) = \nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(x^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \nabla h_j(x^*) = 0$$

- The *Why*: At the optimum, the weighted sum of constraint gradients must balance the objective gradient.
- The *How*: Taking the partial derivative of $L$ with respect to $x$ and equating to zero.

**ŌæĪ Primal Feasibility**

$$g_i(x^*) \leq 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_j(x^*) = 0, \quad \forall j = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

- The *Why*: The solution must lie within the feasible region.
- The *How*: Direct substitution of $x^*$ into the original constraints.

**Ōæó Dual Feasibility**

$$\lambda_i^* \geq 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

- The *Why*: Inequality constraints can only "push" the optimum away, never "pull" it.
- The *How*: Multipliers for $\leq$-type inequalities must be non-negative.

**ŌæŻ Complementary Slackness**

$$\lambda_i^* g_i(x^*) = 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

- The *Why*: If a constraint is inactive ($g_i < 0$), its multiplier must be zero (no influence on optimum).
- The *How*: Either $\lambda_i = 0$ (inactive) or $g_i(x^*) = 0$ (active).

### 2.3 KKT Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Condition | Mathematical Form | Interpretation |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Stationarity | $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i(x^*) + \sum \mu_j \nabla h_j(x^*) = 0$ | Gradient balance |
| 2 | Primal Feasibility (ineq.) | $g_i(x^*) \leq 0$ | In feasible region |
| 3 | Primal Feasibility (eq.) | $h_j(x^*) = 0$ | On equality surface |
| 4 | Dual Feasibility | $\lambda_i^* \geq 0$ | Non-negative multiplier |
| 5 | Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_i^* \cdot g_i(x^*) = 0$ | Either active or zero multiplier |
| 6 | Lagrangian | $L = f + \sum \lambda_i g_i + \sum \mu_j h_j$ | Augmented objective |
| 7 | Sufficiency (convex) | $f, g_i$ convex; $h_j$ affine $\Rightarrow$ KKT is sufficient | Global minimum |

### 2.4 Special Cases and Constraint Types

**Case A: $\geq$-type constraints** $g_i(x) \geq 0$
Convert to standard form: $-g_i(x) \leq 0$. The corresponding multiplier then has $\lambda_i \leq 0$.

**Case B: Non-negativity constraints** $x_j \geq 0$
Treated as $g_i(x) = -x_j \leq 0$, giving $\lambda_j \geq 0$ in stationarity: $\partial f / \partial x_j + \lambda_j = 0$.

**Case C: Equality constraints only**
KKT reduces to classical Lagrange multiplier method with $\mu_j \in \mathbb{R}$ (unrestricted in sign).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Design:** Minimizing weight subject to stress and displacement constraints.
- **Power Systems:** Economic dispatch with generation capacity limits.
- **Machine Learning:** Support Vector Machines (SVM) use KKT for the optimal separating hyperplane.
- **Process Engineering:** Maximizing yield subject to material balance constraints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of KKT Conditions from the Lagrangian

**Step 1:** Begin with the constrained problem:

$$\min f(x) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(x) \leq 0, \quad h_j(x) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Form the Lagrangian $L$ by adding constraints weighted by multipliers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(x, \lambda, \mu) &= f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j h_j(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The Lagrangian is the unconstrained surrogate of the original problem. If the multipliers were zero, $L$ reduces to $f(x)$.

**Step 3:** Take the gradient of $L$ with respect to $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_x L(x, \lambda, \mu) &= \nabla f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(x) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \nabla h_j(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Set the gradient to zero at the optimum $x^*$ (Stationarity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(x^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \nabla h_j(x^*) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The negative gradient of $f$ (direction of steepest descent) must be expressible as a non-negative combination of the active constraint gradients.

**Step 5:** Impose feasibility and sign conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x^*) &\leq 0, \quad \forall i \\
h_j(x^*) &= 0, \quad \forall j \\
\lambda_i^* &\geq 0, \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Impose complementary slackness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_i^* \cdot g_i(x^*) = 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* This is the algebraic statement that the optimizer has no incentive to "move" along an inactive constraint.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example ŌĆö KKT Application

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \leq 3$, $x_1 \geq 0$, $x_2 \geq 0$.

**Step 1:** Rewrite constraints in standard $\leq 0$ form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1(x) &= x_1 + x_2 - 3 \leq 0 \\
g_2(x) &= -x_1 \leq 0 \\
g_3(x) &= -x_2 \leq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Form the Lagrangian:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 3) + \lambda_2(-x_1) + \lambda_3(-x_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply stationarity $\nabla_x L = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} &= 2(x_1 - 1) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} &= 2(x_2 - 2) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply KKT system. Test the unconstrained point $(1, 2)$:

- $g_1(1, 2) = 1 + 2 - 3 = 0$ ŌåÆ **active**, so $\lambda_1$ may be $\geq 0$.
- $g_2(1, 2) = -1 < 0$ ŌåÆ **inactive**, so $\lambda_2 = 0$.
- $g_3(1, 2) = -2 < 0$ ŌåÆ **inactive**, so $\lambda_3 = 0$.

Substituting into stationarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(1 - 1) + \lambda_1 - 0 &= 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 0 \\
2(2 - 2) + \lambda_1 - 0 &= 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Dual feasibility: $\lambda_1 = 0 \geq 0$ Ō£ō

**Step 6:** All four KKT conditions satisfied. Hence, $(x_1^*, x_2^*) = (1, 2)$ is the **KKT point** with optimal value $f^* = 0$.

**Note:** The constraint $x_1 + x_2 \leq 3$ is *tight* at the optimum but does not influence it (multiplier = 0). This is a classical boundary optimum.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example ŌĆö Boundary-Active Optimum

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = (x_1 - 4)^2 + (x_2 - 4)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \leq 6$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

The unconstrained minimizer is $(4, 4)$, but $4 + 4 = 8 > 6$ ŌĆö infeasible. The optimum must lie on the constraint boundary.

**Step 1:** Assume only $g_1$ active: $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$, $g_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 6 - x_1$.

**Step 2:** Substitute into stationarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(x_1 - 4) + \lambda_1 &= 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 2(4 - x_1) \\
2(x_2 - 4) + \lambda_1 &= 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 2(4 - x_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(4 - x_1) &= 2(4 - x_2) \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Combined with $x_1 + x_2 = 6$: $x_1 = x_2 = 3$.

**Step 4:** Compute multiplier:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 = 2(4 - 3) = 2 \geq 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Optimal solution: $x^* = (3, 3)$ with $f^* = 2$, $\lambda_1^* = 2$.

**Interpretation:** The constraint actively bounds the solution; the multiplier $\lambda_1 = 2$ quantifies the sensitivity of the optimal value to relaxing the constraint (shadow price).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 KKT Conditions Logic Flow

The following block diagram captures the **logical flow** of applying the KKT framework to a constrained nonlinear programming problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start NLP Problem"] --> B["Form Lagrangian L"]
    B --> C["Apply Stationarity dL/dx = 0"]
    C --> D{"Identify Active Constraints"}
    D --> E["Set Inactive Constraints to lambda = 0"]
    D --> F["Set Active Constraints g_i = 0"]
    E --> G["Solve Stationarity System"]
    F --> G
    G --> H{"All KKT Conditions Satisfied?"}
    H --> I["Check Primal Feasibility"]
    I --> J["Check Dual Feasibility lambda >= 0"]
    J --> K{"All Verified?"}
    K --> L["Optimal Solution x* Found"]
    K --> M["Re-examine Constraint Activity"]
    M --> D
```

### 4.2 KKT Conditions Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Problem Input"]
        P1["Objective f x"]
        P2["Inequality g_i x <= 0"]
        P3["Equality h_j x = 0"]
    end

    subgraph KKT["KKT Solver Core"]
        K1["Stationarity Equation"]
        K2["Primal Feasibility"]
        K3["Dual Feasibility"]
        K4["Complementary Slackness"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Solution Output"]
        O1["Optimal x*"]
        O2["Multipliers lambda* and mu*"]
        O3["Shadow Prices / Sensitivity"]
    end

    P1 --> K1
    P2 --> K2
    P3 --> K2
    K1 --> K4
    K2 --> K3
    K3 --> K4
    K4 --> O1
    K4 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

### 4.3 Constraint Activity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["At KKT Point x*"] --> Q1{"g_i x* = 0 ?"}
    Q1 --> Y["Constraint is ACTIVE"]
    Q1 --> N["Constraint is INACTIVE"]
    Y --> R1["lambda_i can be >= 0"]
    N --> R2["lambda_i = 0 by complementary slackness"]
    R1 --> OUT["Stationarity uses this gradient"]
    R2 --> OUT2["Stationarity omits this gradient"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
*State the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) necessary conditions for the following nonlinear programming problem:*

$$\min f(x) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(x) \leq 0, \; i = 1, \dots, m; \quad h_j(x) = 0, \; j = 1, \dots, p$$

**Model Answer:**

The KKT conditions state that if $x^*$ is a local minimum and LICQ holds, then there exist $\lambda^* \in \mathbb{R}^m$, $\mu^* \in \mathbb{R}^p$ such that:

1. $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(x^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j^* \nabla h_j(x^*) = 0$ (Stationarity)
2. $g_i(x^*) \leq 0, \; \forall i$ and $h_j(x^*) = 0, \; \forall j$ (Primal Feasibility)
3. $\lambda_i^* \geq 0, \; \forall i$ (Dual Feasibility)
4. $\lambda_i^* g_i(x^*) = 0, \; \forall i$ (Complementary Slackness)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the significance of complementary slackness in the KKT conditions?*

**Model Answer:**

Complementary slackness is expressed as $\lambda_i^* g_i(x^*) = 0$ for all inequality constraints. Its significance is:

- If a constraint $g_i$ is **inactive** at the optimum ($g_i(x^*) < 0$), then $\lambda_i^* = 0$. The constraint has no influence on the solution.
- If a constraint is **active** ($g_i(x^*) = 0$), then $\lambda_i^* \geq 0$, and the constraint gradient contributes to the stationarity equation.

In essence, it determines **which constraints are binding** at the optimal point and **excludes inactive ones** from the active balance of forces.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Formulate the Lagrangian for the following problem and derive the KKT conditions:*

$$\min f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2$$

subject to $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$, $x_1 \leq 3$, $x_2 \geq 0$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert all constraints to $\leq 0$ form:

- $g_1: x_1 + x_2 - 4 \leq 0$
- $g_2: x_1 - 3 \leq 0$
- $g_3: -x_2 \leq 0$

**Step 2:** Form the Lagrangian:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 4) + \lambda_2(x_1 - 3) + \lambda_3(-x_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Stationarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} &= 2x_1 - 4 + \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 0 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} &= 2x_2 - 6 + \lambda_1 - \lambda_3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** KKT complementary conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 (x_1 + x_2 - 4) &= 0, \quad \lambda_1 \geq 0 \\
\lambda_2 (x_1 - 3) &= 0, \quad \lambda_2 \geq 0 \\
\lambda_3 (-x_2) &= 0, \quad \lambda_3 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Test the unconstrained candidate $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 3$: Check $x_2 \geq 0$ Ō£ō, $x_1 + x_2 = 5 > 4$ Ō£Ś ŌĆö **infeasible**.

So the optimum lies on $g_1$ boundary: $x_1 + x_2 = 4$, with $x_2 > 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_3 = 0$, and $x_1 < 3 \Rightarrow \lambda_2 = 0$.

From stationarity with $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 - 4 + \lambda_1 &= 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 4 - 2x_1 \\
2x_2 - 6 + \lambda_1 &= 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 6 - 2x_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating: $4 - 2x_1 = 6 - 2x_2 \Rightarrow x_2 - x_1 = 1$. With $x_1 + x_2 = 4$: $x_1 = 1.5$, $x_2 = 2.5$, $\lambda_1 = 4 - 3 = 1 \geq 0$ Ō£ō.

**KKT point:** $(x_1^*, x_2^*) = (1.5, 2.5)$, $f^* = 2.25 + 6.25 - 6 - 15 = -12.5$.

---

**(b)** *Using KKT conditions, solve:*

$$\min f(x) = (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_1 + x_2 \leq 4, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Constraints in standard form:

- $g_1: x_1 + x_2 - 4 \leq 0$
- $g_2: -x_1 \leq 0$
- $g_3: -x_2 \leq 0$

**Step 2:** Lagrangian:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 + \lambda_1(x_1 + x_2 - 4) - \lambda_2 x_1 - \lambda_3 x_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Stationarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} &= 2(x_1 - 3) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} &= 2(x_2 - 2) + \lambda_1 - \lambda_3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Unconstrained minimizer is $(3, 2)$. Check feasibility: $3 + 2 = 5 > 4$ ŌĆö infeasible. Test boundary $x_1 + x_2 = 4$, with $x_1, x_2 > 0$ (so $\lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 0$).

Stationarity reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(x_1 - 3) + \lambda_1 &= 0 \\
2(x_2 - 2) + \lambda_1 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting: $2(x_1 - x_2) - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 - x_2 = 1$. With $x_1 + x_2 = 4$: $x_1 = 2.5$, $x_2 = 1.5$, $\lambda_1 = 2(3 - 2.5) = 1 \geq 0$ Ō£ō.

**Optimal solution:** $x^* = (2.5, 1.5)$, $f^* = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5$.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper ŌĆö Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** *State and explain the KKT conditions. Discuss why LICQ (Linear Independence Constraint Qualification) is required for KKT to hold. Provide a counterexample where KKT fails without constraint qualification.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement of KKT conditions:** (See Section 1.1 above for the full statement.)

**Why LICQ is required:**

LICQ states that the gradients of all active constraints (at $x^*$) are linearly independent. Without this, the multiplier vector may not be uniquely defined, and the KKT system may have no solution even when an optimum exists. Geometrically, LICQ ensures that the feasible set near $x^*$ behaves like a smooth manifold, allowing a well-defined tangent cone.

**Counterexample ŌĆö KKT Failure Without CQ:**

Consider:

$$\min f(x_1, x_2) = x_1$$

subject to:

- $g_1: x_2 - x_1^2 \leq 0$
- $g_2: -x_1^2 - x_2 \leq 0$

At $x^* = (0, 0)$: both constraints are active, with $\nabla g_1 = (0, 1)$ and $\nabla g_2 = (0, -1)$ ŌĆö **linearly dependent**. The KKT system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1, 0) + \lambda_1(0, 1) + \lambda_2(0, -1) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

yields $0 = 0$ in the first component (no solution for $\lambda$). KKT fails, but $x^* = (0, 0)$ is clearly the unique global minimum ($x_1 \geq 0$ by the feasible region: $x_1^2 \leq x_2$ and $x_2 \leq -x_1^2 \leq 0$, so $x_1 = 0$).

---

**(b)** *Verify whether the KKT conditions are satisfied at the point $x^* = (1, 1)$ for the problem:*

$$\min f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2$$

subject to $x_1^2 + x_2^2 \leq 2$ and $x_1 + x_2 \geq 1$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Standardize constraints:

- $g_1: x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 2 \leq 0$
- $g_2: 1 - x_1 - x_2 \leq 0$

**Step 2:** Evaluate at $x^* = (1, 1)$:

- $g_1(1, 1) = 1 + 1 - 2 = 0$ ŌåÆ **active**
- $g_2(1, 1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0$ ŌåÆ **inactive**, so $\lambda_2 = 0$

**Step 3:** Lagrangian with $\lambda_2 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2 + \lambda_1(x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Stationarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_1} &= 2(x_1 - 2) + 2\lambda_1 x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow 2(1 - 2) + 2\lambda_1 (1) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 1 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_2} &= 2(x_2 - 1) + 2\lambda_1 x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow 2(1 - 1) + 2(1)(1) = 2 \neq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The stationarity condition fails in the $x_2$ component. Therefore, **KKT conditions are NOT satisfied** at $(1, 1)$.

The point is on the boundary but not at the optimum. The true minimum is found by projecting the unconstrained minimizer $(2, 1)$ onto the circle $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = 2$, yielding $x^* \approx (1.265, 0.811)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students often confuse the sign of $\lambda$ for $\geq$-type constraints. Remember: convert $g \geq 0$ to $-g \leq 0$ first.
> 2. **Skipping Complementary Slackness Check:** The KKT system has **4** parts ŌĆö students who solve only stationarity lose up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Dual Feasibility:** Always check $\lambda \geq 0$ at the end. A negative multiplier means the wrong constraint set was assumed active.
> 4. **Misinterpreting Inactive Constraints:** If $g_i(x^*) < 0$, the multiplier is **forced to zero** ŌĆö do not include its gradient in stationarity.
> 5. **Failing to Verify LICQ:** For non-convex problems, LICQ failure can invalidate the KKT result.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **KKT Conditions** are **first-order necessary conditions** for a local optimum in a constrained NLP problem under **LICQ**.
- The **Lagrangian** $L(x, \lambda, \mu)$ is formed by augmenting $f(x)$ with $\lambda_i g_i(x)$ and $\mu_j h_j(x)$.
- The **four KKT conditions** are: **Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility, Complementary Slackness**.
- For $\leq 0$ constraints, $\lambda_i \geq 0$. For $\geq 0$ constraints, transform to standard form first.
- **Complementary slackness** is the key bridge that determines which constraints are active.
- **Sufficiency Theorem:** If $f$ and $g_i$ are **convex** and $h_j$ are **affine**, then KKT is **sufficient** for global optimality.
- **LICQ Failure** can cause KKT to fail to identify the optimum (counterexample: $g_1 = x_2 - x_1^2$ and $g_2 = -x_1^2 - x_2$ at the origin).
- **Shadow Price:** The value of $\lambda_i^*$ indicates the rate of change in $f^*$ per unit relaxation of constraint $i$.
- For equality-only constraints, KKT reduces to **classical Lagrange multiplier method**.
- KKT is the **foundation** of convex optimization, SVMs, economic dispatch, and structural optimization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Programming Problems and Game Theory - Mathematical Formulation of LPP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET524 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Programming Problems and Game Theory - Mathematical Formulation of LPP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Programming Problem (LPP):** A linear programming problem is a mathematical optimization model designed to **maximize or minimize** a linear objective function subject to a finite set of **linear equality and inequality constraints** over real-valued decision variables.

Mathematically, an LPP seeks to determine the vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$ that **optimizes**:

$$
\text{Optimize } Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n
$$

subject to the constraint system:

$$
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} \; \{ \leq,\; =,\; \geq \} \; \mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{x} \geq 0
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the cost/profit coefficient vector
- $\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the technological coefficient matrix
- $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the resource requirement vector
- $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}_{\geq 0}$ is the vector of decision variables

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Bakery Production Manager"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Intuition (The Bakery Story):**
> Imagine you are the manager of a bakery that produces **two products** ŌĆö *Sponge Cake* ($x_1$) and *Bread Loaf* ($x_2$). Each cake gives a profit of Ōé╣**40**, each loaf gives Ōé╣**25**. You have only **300 kg of flour**, **40 hours of oven time**, and the oven cannot produce negative items. The question is: **How many cakes and loaves should you bake tomorrow to earn the maximum profit, without running out of resources?**
>
> This is exactly what an LPP does ŌĆö it picks the **best combination of decisions** when resources are **finite, linear, and additive**. The word *"linear"* means every relationship (profit, resource use) scales proportionally (doubling the cake doubles the flour used). The word *"programming"* refers to *planning*, not computer programming.

### 1.3 Key Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Every LPP:**
> 1. **Decision Variables** ŌĆö *What* can I control? (e.g., quantity of product A, B)
> 2. **Objective Function** ŌĆö *What* am I optimizing? (e.g., maximize profit = $c_1x_1 + c_2x_2$)
> 3. **Constraints** ŌĆö *What* limits me? (e.g., labor, material, time, demand)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Structural Components of an LPP

Every KTU-style LPP formulation must explicitly identify these six elements. Examiners frequently award partial marks (2 out of 14) just for correctly listing them.

| # | Component | Mathematical Symbol | Plain-English Description |
|---|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | **Decision Variables** | $x_j \; (j=1,2,\ldots,n)$ | Quantities we control |
| 2 | **Objective Function** | $Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | The goal to maximize/minimize |
| 3 | **Constraints** | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \; \{ \leq, =, \geq \} \; b_i$ | Resource / policy restrictions |
| 4 | **Non-Negativity** | $x_j \geq 0$ | Physical impossibility of negatives |
| 5 | **Parameters** | $c_j, a_{ij}, b_i$ | Known, fixed constants |
| 6 | **Feasible Region** | $S = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \mid \mathbf{Ax} \leq \mathbf{b}, \; \mathbf{x} \geq 0\}$ | Set of all valid solutions |

### 2.2 The Step-by-Step Logical Framework (Why & How)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify Decision Variables**
- **Why:** The variable choice must capture *every controllable quantity*. If you bake cakes and bread, you cannot combine them into a single variable ŌĆö the profit coefficients differ.
- **How:** Read the problem statement, underline every noun representing an "amount," "units," "quantity," or "how much."

**Step 2 ŌĆö Construct the Objective Function**
- **Why:** The optimization target is the heart of the LPP. Mixing "minimize cost" with "maximize revenue" without conversion is a common KTU valuation pitfall.
- **How:** Express the goal as a **linear sum** of decision variables multiplied by per-unit profit/cost. Use $Z_{\max}$ for maximization, $Z_{\min}$ for minimization.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Formulate the Constraint System**
- **Why:** Real resources are finite. Each constraint represents a *resource cap*, a *demand floor*, a *ratio condition*, or a *mixing rule*.
- **How:** For each resource, compute *total consumption = sum (per-unit usage ├Ś variable)*. Compare against the *available amount* using $\leq, =, \geq$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Non-Negativity Restrictions**
- **Why:** Negative production is physically meaningless in nearly all engineering/economic contexts.
- **How:** Append $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \geq 0$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Verify Linearity**
- **Why:** If any term becomes $x_1 \cdot x_2$, $x_1^2$, $\sqrt{x_1}$, or $\log x_1$, the problem is **no longer an LPP** ŌĆö it becomes NLP, MILP, etc.
- **How:** Check that all expressions are of the form $\sum c_j x_j$ and $\sum a_{ij} x_j$ ŌĆö i.e., **first-degree, additive**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Convert to Standard / Canonical Form** *(if required by solution method)*
- **Standard Form:** All constraints are *equalities* with slack/surplus variables, objective is *maximization*.
- **Canonical Form:** All constraints are $\leq$ type with $x_j \geq 0$, objective is *maximization*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Form | Purpose |
|---------|----------------|---------|
| General LPP | $\max/\min Z = \mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$, s.t. $\mathbf{Ax} \leq \mathbf{b}, \; \mathbf{x} \geq 0$ | Compact representation |
| Slack Variable (for $\leq$) | $\sum a_{ij} x_j + s_i = b_i$ | Converts $\leq$ into $=$ |
| Surplus Variable (for $\geq$) | $\sum a_{ij} x_j - s_i = b_i$ | Converts $\geq$ into $=$ |
| Artificial Variable | $a_i \geq 0$ | Used in Big-M / Two-Phase methods |
| Standard Form | Maximize $Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$, s.t. $\mathbf{Ax} = \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{x} \geq 0$ | Required input to Simplex |
| Canonical Form | Maximize $Z = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$, s.t. $\mathbf{Ax} \leq \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{x} \geq 0$ | Used in theoretical proofs |
| Dual Variables (shadow price) | $y_i$ associated with constraint $i$ | Economic interpretation of resources |
| Duality Theorem (Key) | $\max Z = \min W$ (if both feasible & bounded) | Foundation of duality theory |

### 2.4 Four Proportionality & Additivity Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Anchor:** The model is valid only when these four conditions hold:
> 1. **Proportionality** ŌĆö Contribution of $x_j$ to objective/constraint is proportional to $x_j$ itself.
> 2. **Additivity** ŌĆö Total contribution = sum of individual contributions (no interaction terms).
> 3. **Certainty** ŌĆö All parameters $c_j, a_{ij}, b_i$ are known constants.
> 4. **Divisibility** ŌĆö Decision variables may take any real (fractional) value. *(If integer-only, it becomes an Integer LPP ŌĆö ILPP.)*

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Production Planning:** Maximize profit in a factory with limited raw material and machine hours.
- **Diet / Blending Problems:** Minimize cost of animal feed subject to minimum nutritional content.
- **Transportation:** Minimize shipping cost from $m$ warehouses to $n$ customers.
- **Assignment & Scheduling:** Allocate jobs to machines/workers to minimize time/cost.
- **Network Flow & Telecommunication Routing:** Optimize bandwidth allocation.
- **Refinery Yield Optimization:** Maximize gasoline/diesel output given crude oil composition.
- **Portfolio Optimization:** Maximize returns subject to risk and budget constraints.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 ŌĆö The Classical Production Problem (KTU Frequently Repeated)

> **Problem Statement (Verbatim from KTU Style):**
> A furniture company manufactures **Tables** and **Chairs**. Each Table requires **2 hours of carpentry** and **1 hour of painting**, yielding a profit of **Ōé╣80**. Each Chair requires **1 hour of carpentry** and **1 hour of painting**, yielding a profit of **Ōé╣40**. The available carpentry hours are **10**, and painting hours are **6**. Formulate the LPP to maximize profit.

#### Step-by-Step Mathematical Formulation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define Decision Variables**

Let:
- $x_1$ = number of Tables produced
- $x_2$ = number of Chairs produced

**Step 2 ŌĆö Identify Profit Coefficients ($c_j$)**
- Each Table contributes Ōé╣80 ŌåÆ $c_1 = 80$
- Each Chair contributes Ōé╣40 ŌåÆ $c_2 = 40$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Construct the Objective Function**

Total profit $Z$ equals the sum of (profit per unit ├Ś quantity):

$$
Z = 80x_1 + 40x_2
$$

Since the company aims to **maximize** profit, the objective is:

$$
\text{Maximize } Z = 80x_1 + 40x_2
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Formulate the Constraints**

*Resource: Carpentry Hours*

$$
\underbrace{2x_1}_{\text{carpentry for tables}} + \underbrace{1 \cdot x_2}_{\text{carpentry for chairs}} \leq \underbrace{10}_{\text{available hours}}
$$

$$
2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10
$$

*Resource: Painting Hours*

$$
\underbrace{1 \cdot x_1}_{\text{painting for tables}} + \underbrace{1 \cdot x_2}_{\text{painting for chairs}} \leq \underbrace{6}_{\text{available hours}}
$$

$$
x_1 + x_2 \leq 6
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Non-Negativity**

Since negative production is impossible:

$$
x_1 \geq 0, \quad x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Mathematical Model**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } \quad & Z = 80x_1 + 40x_2 \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad & 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10 \\
& x_1 + x_2 \leq 6 \\
& x_1, \, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Verification of Linearity**

Every term in the objective and constraints is of degree 1 in the variables, and the coefficients are constants. Ō£ō Valid LPP.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 ŌĆö Diet / Blending Problem (Surplus Variable Conversion)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A dietician wishes to mix two types of foods ŌĆö *Food A* and *Food B* ŌĆö to create a diet that provides **at least 8 units of Vitamin X** and **at least 10 units of Vitamin Y**. Each kg of Food A gives 1 unit of X and 2 units of Y at a cost of Ōé╣4. Each kg of Food B gives 3 units of X and 1 unit of Y at a cost of Ōé╣6. Formulate the LPP to **minimize cost**.

#### Step-by-Step Mathematical Formulation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decision Variables**

Let:
- $x_1$ = kilograms of Food A used
- $x_2$ = kilograms of Food B used

**Step 2 ŌĆö Cost Coefficients**

- Cost per kg of A: Ōé╣4 ŌåÆ $c_1 = 4$
- Cost per kg of B: Ōé╣6 ŌåÆ $c_2 = 6$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Objective Function (Minimize Cost)**

$$
\text{Minimize } Z = 4x_1 + 6x_2
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Vitamin Constraints (Ōēź Type)**

*Vitamin X (at least 8 units):*

$$
1 \cdot x_1 + 3x_2 \geq 8
$$

*Vitamin Y (at least 10 units):*

$$
2x_1 + 1 \cdot x_2 \geq 10
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Non-Negativity**

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Model**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } \quad & Z = 4x_1 + 6x_2 \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad & x_1 + 3x_2 \geq 8 \\
& 2x_1 + x_2 \geq 10 \\
& x_1, \, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Conversion to Standard Form (KTU Frequently Asked Follow-up)**

To convert $\geq$ into $=$, we **subtract a surplus variable** $s_i \geq 0$ and add an **artificial variable** $a_i \geq 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 + 3x_2 - s_1 + a_1 &= 8 \\
2x_1 + x_2 - s_2 + a_2 &= 10 \\
x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2, a_1, a_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Example 3 ŌĆö Manufacturing with Multiple Product Lines

> **Problem Statement:**
> A company produces three products $P_1, P_2, P_3$ using two machines $M_1$ and $M_2$. The processing time (in hours) per unit and the available machine hours are:

| Product | $M_1$ (hrs/unit) | $M_2$ (hrs/unit) | Profit (Ōé╣/unit) |
|---------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| $P_1$ | 2 | 3 | 50 |
| $P_2$ | 4 | 2 | 60 |
| $P_3$ | 1 | 5 | 40 |
| **Available Hours** | **100** | **150** | ŌĆö |

Additionally, the company must produce **at least 10 units of $P_1$** due to a contract. Formulate the LPP.

#### Step-by-Step Mathematical Formulation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Decision Variables**

$$
x_1, x_2, x_3 = \text{number of units of } P_1, P_2, P_3 \text{ produced, respectively.}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Objective Function (Profit Maximization)**

$$
\text{Maximize } Z = 50x_1 + 60x_2 + 40x_3
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Constraints**

*Machine $M_1$ time constraint:*

$$
2x_1 + 4x_2 + 1x_3 \leq 100
$$

*Machine $M_2$ time constraint:*

$$
3x_1 + 2x_2 + 5x_3 \leq 150
$$

*Contractual demand for $P_1$:*

$$
x_1 \geq 10
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Non-Negativity**

$$
x_1, x_2, x_3 \geq 0
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final Model**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } \quad & Z = 50x_1 + 60x_2 + 40x_3 \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad & 2x_1 + 4x_2 + x_3 \leq 100 \\
& 3x_1 + 2x_2 + 5x_3 \leq 150 \\
& x_1 \geq 10 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Standard vs. Canonical Form Conversion

| Original Form | Conversion Action | Resulting Form |
|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| $a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i$ | Add slack $s_i \geq 0$: $a_{ij}x_j + s_i = b_i$ | Equation with $s_i \geq 0$ |
| $a_{ij} x_j \geq b_i$ | Subtract surplus $s_i \geq 0$: $a_{ij}x_j - s_i = b_i$ | Equation with $s_i \geq 0$ |
| $x_j$ unrestricted | Replace $x_j = x_j^{+} - x_j^{-}$, both $\geq 0$ | Two non-negative variables |
| Minimize $Z$ | Replace $\min Z$ with $\max (-Z)$ | Standard maximization form |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LPP Formulation

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **six-step formulation workflow** required by KTU examiners. This serves as a memory anchor for answering 14-mark formulation problems in the End Semester Examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Decision Variables x1 x2 ... xn]
    B --> C[Identify Parameters cj aij bi]
    C --> D[Construct Objective Function Z = sum cj xj]
    D --> E{Is Goal Maximize or Minimize?}
    E -->|Maximize| F1[Write Maximize Z]
    E -->|Minimize| F2[Write Minimize Z]
    F1 --> G[Formulate Constraints]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Resource Limits: less than or equal b_i]
    G --> I[Minimum Demand: greater than or equal b_i]
    G --> J[Mixing Rule: equality b_i]
    H --> K[Add Non-Negativity xj greater or equal 0]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Verify Linearity and Additivity]
    L --> M{Valid LPP?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Final Mathematical Model Boxed]
    M -->|No| O[Check: Nonlinear terms or Integer requirements]
    O --> P[Convert to NLP or ILPP as appropriate]
    N --> Q[Optional: Convert to Standard Form using Slack or Surplus Variables]
    Q --> R[END: Ready for Graphical or Simplex Method]
```

### 4.2 Component Architecture of an LPP

This diagram illustrates how the **six components** of an LPP interact and feed into the optimization engine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        DV[Decision Variables xj]
        PR[Parameters cj aij bi]
    end
    subgraph CoreModel[Core LPP Model]
        OBJ[Objective Function Z]
        CON[Constraint System Ax less or equal b]
        NN[Non-Negativity xj greater or equal 0]
    end
    subgraph SolutionLayer[Solution Output]
        OPT[Optimal Solution x star]
        ZVAL[Optimal Value Z star]
        SHAD[Shadow Prices from Dual]
    end
    DV --> OBJ
    DV --> CON
    PR --> OBJ
    PR --> CON
    OBJ --> OPT
    CON --> OPT
    NN --> OPT
    OPT --> ZVAL
    OPT --> SHAD
```

### 4.3 Standard Form Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[General LPP Form] --> B{Constraint Type?}
    B -->|less or equal| C[Add Slack Variable si greater or equal 0]
    B -->|greater or equal| D[Subtract Surplus Variable si greater or equal 0]
    B -->|equal| E[Keep As Is]
    C --> F[Add Artificial Variable if needed]
    D --> F
    E --> G{Variable Sign?}
    G -->|xj greater or equal 0| H[Keep xj]
    G -->|xj unrestricted| I[Replace xj = xj positive minus xj negative]
    F --> J[Standard Form Maximize Z s.t. Ax equal b x greater or equal 0]
    I --> J
    H --> J
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*Define a Linear Programming Problem. List any four characteristics of an LPP.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition:** A Linear Programming Problem is a mathematical optimization technique used to find the best possible outcome (maximum profit or minimum cost) from a set of available resources, where both the objective and constraints are expressed as **linear functions** of the decision variables.
>
> **Four Characteristics:**
> 1. **Objective Function** ŌĆö A linear function to be optimized.
> 2. **Constraints** ŌĆö A set of linear inequalities or equalities.
> 3. **Non-Negativity** ŌĆö Decision variables cannot be negative ($x_j \geq 0$).
> 4. **Finiteness** ŌĆö The feasible region is bounded by a finite number of constraints.
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four characteristics.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Slack Variable, Surplus Variable, and Artificial Variable with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Variable Type | Symbol | Used With | Purpose | Example |
|---------------|--------|-----------|---------|---------|
| Slack | $s_i \geq 0$ | $\leq$ constraint | Converts $\leq$ to $=$ | $2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 10$ |
| Surplus | $s_i \geq 0$ | $\geq$ constraint | Converts $\geq$ to $=$ | $x_1 + 3x_2 - s_1 = 8$ |
| Artificial | $a_i \geq 0$ | Starting basis | Provides an initial BFS | $x_1 + 3x_2 - s_1 + a_1 = 8$ |

*(Award 1 mark per row of correct explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 2 ŌĆö Standard Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö July 2024]**

**(a)** A company manufactures two products $A$ and $B$. The profit per unit is Ōé╣**50** for $A$ and Ōé╣**70** for $B$. Product $A$ requires **3 hours of machine time** and Product $B$ requires **5 hours**. The total available machine time is **150 hours per week**. Additionally, the company must produce **at least 10 units of $B$** to meet market demand, and the total production capacity is restricted to a maximum of **40 units of $A$**. Formulate the LPP mathematically to maximize profit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Convert the formulated LPP from part (a) into **standard form** suitable for applying the Simplex method. Clearly identify all slack, surplus, and artificial variables introduced. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Mathematical Formulation (7 Marks)**

*Step 1 ŌĆö Decision Variables:*
- $x_1$ = number of units of Product A produced per week
- $x_2$ = number of units of Product B produced per week

*Step 2 ŌĆö Objective Function:*

$$
\text{Maximize } Z = 50x_1 + 70x_2
$$

*Step 3 ŌĆö Constraints:*

Machine time constraint ($\leq$):

$$
3x_1 + 5x_2 \leq 150
$$

Minimum demand for $B$ ($\geq$):

$$
x_2 \geq 10
$$

Maximum production of $A$ ($\leq$):

$$
x_1 \leq 40
$$

*Step 4 ŌĆö Non-Negativity:*

$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

*Final Mathematical Model (Boxed):*

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } \quad & Z = 50x_1 + 70x_2 \\
\text{Subject to:} \quad & 3x_1 + 5x_2 \leq 150 \\
& x_2 \geq 10 \\
& x_1 \leq 40 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Marking Distribution (Part a):** Decision Variables ŌĆö 1.5 marks | Objective ŌĆö 1.5 marks | Constraints ŌĆö 3 marks | Non-Negativity ŌĆö 1 mark.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Standard Form Conversion (7 Marks)**

The standard form requires **all constraints to be equalities** with a **maximization objective** and **non-negative variables**.

*Step 1 ŌĆö Convert $\leq$ constraints using Slack Variables:*

$$
3x_1 + 5x_2 + s_1 = 150 \quad (\text{machine time})
$$

$$
x_1 + s_2 = 40 \quad (\text{maximum } A)
$$

*Step 2 ŌĆö Convert $\geq$ constraints using Surplus + Artificial Variables:*

$$
x_2 - s_3 + a_1 = 10 \quad (\text{minimum } B)
$$

*Step 3 ŌĆö Final Standard Form:*

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
