<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Optimization of Fuzzy Systems ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy System Optimization** is the systematic procedure of tuning the parameters of a fuzzy inference system (FIS) ŌĆö namely the **membership function parameters** (shape, support, peak position), the **rule base**, and the **defuzzification parameters** ŌĆö using a mathematical optimization algorithm so that the system minimizes (or maximizes) a chosen performance criterion such as approximation error, classification error, or a control cost function.

A fuzzy system can be expressed as a mapping $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^p$ whose parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} = \{c_{ij}, \sigma_{ij}, w_k\}$ (centres, spreads, and rule weights) are adjusted by solving:

$$\min_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \; J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big( f_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(x_i),\, y_i \big)$$

where $J$ is the **loss function** and $\mathcal{L}$ is a per-sample penalty (typically squared error $\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$).

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 specifically links **fuzzy parameter tuning** to **gradient-based and evolutionary optimizers**, and to the **neuro-fuzzy** paradigm where a neural network learns the fuzzy parameters from data.

## 1.2 Neural Network Based Optimization ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neural Network Based Optimization (NNBO)** is the process of using an artificial neural network (ANN) ŌĆö or a network of interconnected processing units (neurons) ŌĆö as a computational engine to find the optimal values of a system's parameters. The ANN learns a mapping from candidate solutions to objective values, or it directly performs gradient-based weight adaptation to solve a constrained/unconstrained optimization problem.

Mathematically, given an objective $F(\mathbf{x})$, the ANN iteratively updates its weight matrix $\mathbf{W}^{(l)}$ for layer $l$ using:

$$\mathbf{W}^{(l)}_{t+1} = \mathbf{W}^{(l)}_{t} - \eta_t \, \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{W}^{(l)}_{t}}$$

where $\eta_t$ is the **learning rate** and $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

### Analogy 1: The "Expert Chef Tuning a Recipe" (Fuzzy Optimization)

> Imagine a chef has a **fuzzy recipe** ŌĆö instead of saying *"add 200 g of salt"*, the recipe says *"add a small amount of salt, around a handful"*. This is a fuzzy rule. Now, the chef wants to make the dish taste **just right** for a particular customer. They adjust the "small" and "handful" by tasting repeatedly.

**Optimization of fuzzy systems** is exactly this ŌĆö *adjusting the vague, linguistic rules and membership shapes so that the system output matches the desired output as closely as possible.* The "tasting" is the loss function, and the "adjusting" is the optimization algorithm.

### Analogy 2: The "Student Learning from Mistakes" (Neural Network Optimization)

> A child learning to ride a bicycle falls, analyses which way they tilted, adjusts their balance, and tries again. Each attempt makes them slightly better. A neural network does the same: it makes a prediction, **measures the error**, and **adjusts its internal weights** so that next time the error is smaller.

**Neural network based optimization** mirrors this feedback loop: predict ŌåÆ measure error ŌåÆ adjust weights ŌåÆ repeat. The "fall" is the forward-pass output error, and the "balance correction" is the back-propagation gradient update.

## 1.4 Why Combine Fuzzy Systems with Neural Networks?

> [!NOTE]
> **Neuro-Fuzzy Systems** combine the *human-like reasoning* of fuzzy logic with the *learning capability* of neural networks. Fuzzy systems are interpretable but hard to tune; neural networks are excellent learners but act as black boxes. Their integration yields systems that **learn from data** (neural) while remaining **linguistically interpretable** (fuzzy).

The most widely studied neuro-fuzzy architecture is **ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System)**, proposed by **Jang (1993)**. It uses a **5-layer feedforward network** to implement a **TakagiŌĆōSugenoŌĆōKang (TSK) fuzzy system**, with parameters optimized by a **hybrid learning rule** (least-squares + gradient descent).

## 1.5 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | KTU Symbol |
|------|---------|------------|
| Membership Function | Degree of belongingness of $x$ to fuzzy set $A$ | $\mu_A(x) \in [0,1]$ |
| Fuzzy Rule | IFŌĆōTHEN statement with linguistic antecedent/consequent | $R_i: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_i \text{ THEN } y \text{ is } B_i$ |
| Artificial Neuron | Computational unit mimicking biological neuron | $y = \phi\big(\sum w_i x_i + b\big)$ |
| Activation Function | Non-linear transformation inside a neuron | $\sigma(\cdot),\, \tanh(\cdot),\, \text{ReLU}(\cdot)$ |
| Epoch | One complete pass through the training data | ŌĆö |
| Convergence | State where loss stops decreasing appreciably | $\|\nabla J\| < \epsilon$ |

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model of an Artificial Neuron

An artificial neuron (the **McCullochŌĆōPitts model** extended with real-valued weights and an activation function) computes:

$$u_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ki} \, x_i + b_k$$

$$y_k = \varphi(u_k)$$

where:

- $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ are the **inputs**
- $w_{k1}, w_{k2}, \dots, w_{kn}$ are the **synaptic weights**
- $b_k$ is the **bias** term
- $u_k$ is the **net input / activation potential**
- $\varphi(\cdot)$ is the **activation function**
- $y_k$ is the **output** of the neuron

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bias** $b_k$ shifts the activation function horizontally; it is critical because without bias, the decision boundary (hyperplane) is forced to pass through the origin, severely limiting expressivity.

## 2.2 Common Activation Functions

### (a) Step (Heaviside) Function

$$\varphi(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } u \geq 0 \\ 0 & \text{if } u < 0 \end{cases}$$

Used in the original **Rosenblatt perceptron (1958)**. Disadvantage: not differentiable ŌåÆ cannot be used with gradient descent.

### (b) Sigmoid (Logistic) Function

$$\varphi(u) = \sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$$

Range: $(0, 1)$. **Smooth and differentiable** ŌĆö the workhorse of classical back-propagation.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{du} = \sigma(u)\big(1 - \sigma(u)\big)$$

### (c) Hyperbolic Tangent

$$\varphi(u) = \tanh(u) = \frac{e^{u} - e^{-u}}{e^{u} + e^{-u}}$$

Range: $(-1, 1)$. **Zero-centred** ŌĆö often preferred over sigmoid because it gives faster convergence in deep networks.

### (d) ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit)

$$\varphi(u) = \max(0, u)$$

Range: $[0, \infty)$. **Default choice in modern deep learning** ŌĆö avoids vanishing-gradient problem for $u > 0$.

## 2.3 Network Architecture

### Single-Layer Perceptron
- Input layer + output layer (no hidden layer)
- Can solve only **linearly separable** problems (e.g., AND, OR, but NOT XOR)

### Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)
- One or more **hidden layers** between input and output
- With at least one hidden layer + non-linear activation ŌåÆ **universal function approximator** (Cybenko 1989, Hornik 1991)

$$\mathbf{h}^{(1)} = \varphi\big(\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}^{(1)}\big)$$

$$\mathbf{h}^{(2)} = \varphi\big(\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \mathbf{h}^{(1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(2)}\big)$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}^{(3)} \mathbf{h}^{(2)} + \mathbf{b}^{(3)}$$

## 2.4 Learning Paradigms

| Paradigm | Data Provided | Goal | KTU Use |
|----------|---------------|------|---------|
| **Supervised** | Inputs + desired outputs $(x_i, y_i)$ | Learn mapping $f: x \to y$ | Most common ŌĆö back-prop |
| **Unsupervised** | Only inputs $x_i$ | Discover structure / cluster | Self-organizing maps |
| **Reinforcement** | Reward signal $r$ | Maximize cumulative reward | Control, game playing |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula the KTU examiner can possibly test for this topic. Memorize it line by line.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Net input to neuron $k$ | $u_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ki} x_i + b_k$ | Weighted sum + bias |
| 2 | Output of neuron $k$ | $y_k = \varphi(u_k)$ | After activation |
| 3 | Sigmoid function | $\sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$ | $\varphi$ classical choice |
| 4 | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma'(u) = \sigma(u)(1 - \sigma(u))$ | For back-prop |
| 5 | Tanh derivative | $\tanh'(u) = 1 - \tanh^2(u)$ | For back-prop |
| 6 | ReLU derivative | $\text{ReLU}'(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & u > 0 \\ 0 & u \le 0 \end{cases}$ | For back-prop |
| 7 | Mean Squared Error | $E = \frac{1}{2N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(d_i - y_i)^2$ | Loss function |
| 8 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $E = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[d_i \ln y_i + (1-d_i)\ln(1-y_i)\big]$ | For classification |
| 9 | Perceptron Rule | $w_k^{\text{new}} = w_k^{\text{old}} + \eta(d - y) x_k$ | Single-layer update |
| 10 | Delta (LMS) Rule | $\Delta w_{ki} = \eta (d_k - y_k) x_i$ | WidrowŌĆōHoff |
| 11 | Gradient Descent | $w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta \nabla E(w_t)$ | Generic weight update |
| 12 | Back-prop: Output layer | $\delta_k^{(L)} = (d_k - y_k) \, \varphi'(u_k^{(L)})$ | Error signal |
| 13 | Back-prop: Hidden layer | $\delta_j^{(\ell)} = \varphi'(u_j^{(\ell)}) \sum_{k} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)}$ | Chain rule propagation |
| 14 | Momentum Update | $\Delta w_t = \eta \nabla E + \alpha \Delta w_{t-1}$ | Accelerates convergence |
| 15 | Adaptive LR (AdaGrad) | $w_{t+1} = w_t - \frac{\eta}{\sqrt{G_t + \epsilon}} \nabla E$ | Per-parameter scaling |
| 16 | Fuzzy centroid (defuzz) | $y^* = \frac{\sum_{i} \mu_i \, \bar{y}_i}{\sum_{i} \mu_i}$ | Output crisp value |
| 17 | Gaussian MF | $\mu(x) = \exp\!\big(-\frac{1}{2}\big(\frac{x - c}{\sigma}\big)^2\big)$ | Most-used MF shape |
| 18 | ANFIS consequent | $f_i = p_i x + q_i y + r_i$ | TSK linear consequent |
| 19 | ANFIS firing strength | $w_i = \mu_{A_i}(x) \cdot \mu_{B_i}(y)$ | Rule node output |
| 20 | ANFIS normalized firing | $\bar{w}_i = \frac{w_i}{\sum_j w_j}$ | Normalization layer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility** ŌĆö These formulas are the backbone of every modern application: *predictive control* (tuning controller gains), *fault diagnosis* (classifying fault signatures), *image recognition* (CNN feature extraction), *time-series forecasting* (RNN/LSTM), and *reinforcement learning* (policy networks in robotics).

## 2.6 The Optimization Connection

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to recognize that **training a neural network is itself an optimization problem**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Training a neural network = solving** $\min_{\mathbf{W}} E(\mathbf{W})$ **where** $E$ **is the loss function and** $\mathbf{W}$ **is the concatenation of all weight matrices and bias vectors across all layers.**

Hence, every classical optimization technique (gradient descent, Newton, conjugate gradient, LevenbergŌĆōMarquardt, quasi-Newton BFGS, Adam) is directly applicable. This is the **fundamental bridge** between classical optimization theory and modern machine learning that KTU examiners love to test.

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Perceptron Learning Rule

### Setup

Consider a single neuron with weight vector $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n)^T$, input vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$, and a step activation $\varphi(u) = \text{sign}(u)$. The desired output for input $\mathbf{x}^{(p)}$ is $d^{(p)} \in \{-1, +1\}$.

We want to find $\mathbf{w}$ such that the perceptron classifies every training sample correctly. The misclassification condition is:

$$y^{(p)} \neq d^{(p)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad d^{(p)} \, \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)} < 0$$

### Constructing the Loss

Define the **perceptron criterion function** (a piecewise-linear loss penalizing misclassification):

$$E_p(\mathbf{w}) = \max\big(0, \; -d^{(p)} \, \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)}\big)$$

When the sample is correctly classified, $-d^{(p)} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \le 0$ and the loss is $0$. When misclassified, the loss is positive and we need to decrease it.

### Gradient Computation

The gradient of $E_p$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$, for a misclassified sample, is:

$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -d^{(p)} \, \mathbf{x}^{(p)}$$

### Update Rule (Gradient Descent Step)

Apply gradient descent $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta \nabla E_p$:

$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{w}_{\text{old}} - \eta \big(-d^{(p)} \mathbf{x}^{(p)}\big)$$

$$\boxed{\; \mathbf{w}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{w}_{\text{old}} + \eta \, d^{(p)} \, \mathbf{x}^{(p)} \;}$$

Component-wise (writing only the misclassified sample update):

$$w_i^{\text{new}} = w_i^{\text{old}} + \eta \, (d - y) \, x_i$$

This is the **Perceptron Learning Rule** of Rosenblatt (1958).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Theorem (Novikoff, 1962):** If the training data is linearly separable with margin $\gamma > 0$ and $\|\mathbf{x}\| \le R$, then the perceptron algorithm makes at most $(R/\gamma)^2$ mistakes before convergence.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Back-Propagation Algorithm

### Step 1: Define the Network and Loss

Consider a feedforward MLP with layers $\ell = 1, 2, \dots, L$. For layer $\ell$:

$$\mathbf{u}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \, \mathbf{y}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}$$

$$\mathbf{y}^{(\ell)} = \varphi^{(\ell)}\big(\mathbf{u}^{(\ell)}\big)$$

For the output layer $\ell = L$, $\mathbf{y}^{(L)} = \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is the prediction. Define the loss for a single sample using Mean Squared Error:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{n_L} \big(d_k - y_k^{(L)}\big)^2$$

### Step 2: Local Gradient at the Output Layer

The local gradient (error signal) for neuron $k$ in output layer $L$:

$$\delta_k^{(L)} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_k^{(L)}}$$

Apply the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial u_k^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k^{(L)}}{\partial u_k^{(L)}}$$

We have:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k^{(L)}} = -(d_k - y_k^{(L)})$$

$$\frac{\partial y_k^{(L)}}{\partial u_k^{(L)}} = \varphi'^{(L)}\big(u_k^{(L)}\big)$$

Therefore:

$$\delta_k^{(L)} = -(d_k - y_k^{(L)}) \cdot \varphi'^{(L)}\big(u_k^{(L)}\big)$$

For MSE loss with linear output ($\varphi^{(L)}(u) = u$ so $\varphi'^{(L)} = 1$):

$$\delta_k^{(L)} = (y_k^{(L)} - d_k)$$

### Step 3: Back-Propagate the Error to Hidden Layers

For hidden layer $\ell < L$, neuron $j$ receives error from all neurons $k$ in layer $\ell+1$:

$$\delta_j^{(\ell)} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n_{\ell+1}} \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_k^{(\ell+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}}$$

Since $u_k^{(\ell+1)} = \sum_j w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} y_j^{(\ell)} + b_k^{(\ell+1)} = \sum_j w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \varphi^{(\ell)}(u_j^{(\ell)}) + b_k^{(\ell+1)}$:

$$\frac{\partial u_k^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}} = w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \, \varphi'^{(\ell)}(u_j^{(\ell)})$$

Substituting $\partial E / \partial u_k^{(\ell+1)} = \delta_k^{(\ell+1)}$:

$$\boxed{\; \delta_j^{(\ell)} = \varphi'^{(\ell)}\big(u_j^{(\ell)}\big) \sum_{k=1}^{n_{\ell+1}} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} \, w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \;}$$

### Step 4: Compute Weight Gradients

For weight $w_{ji}^{(\ell)}$ (connecting neuron $i$ in layer $\ell-1$ to neuron $j$ in layer $\ell$):

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ji}^{(\ell)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}}{\partial w_{ji}^{(\ell)}} = \delta_j^{(\ell)} \cdot y_i^{(\ell-1)}$$

### Step 5: Update the Weights

Apply gradient descent:

$$\boxed{\; w_{ji}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow w_{ji}^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(\ell)} \, y_i^{(\ell-1)} \;}$$

This is the **canonical back-propagation update**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Sigmoid Output and its Derivative

Starting from $\sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{du} = \frac{d}{du}\big(1 + e^{-u}\big)^{-1}$$

$$= -\big(1 + e^{-u}\big)^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-u})$$

$$= \frac{e^{-u}}{(1 + e^{-u})^2}$$

Now factor:

$$= \frac{(1 + e^{-u}) - 1}{(1 + e^{-u})^2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}} - \frac{1}{(1 + e^{-u})^2}$$

$$= \sigma(u) - \sigma^2(u)$$

$$= \sigma(u)\big(1 - \sigma(u)\big)$$

Therefore $\sigma'(u) = \sigma(u)(1 - \sigma(u))$ ŌĆö a result critical to the back-prop derivation.

## 3.4 ANFIS Architecture ŌĆö Layer-by-Layer Analysis

**ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System)** implements a **first-order TakagiŌĆōSugenoŌĆōKang (TSK) fuzzy system** as a 5-layer adaptive network. Consider a fuzzy system with two inputs $x, y$ and one output $f$, with two rules:

$$\text{Rule 1: } \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B_1 \text{ THEN } f_1 = p_1 x + q_1 y + r_1$$

$$\text{Rule 2: } \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_2 \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B_2 \text{ THEN } f_2 = p_2 x + q_2 y + r_2$$

### Layer 1 ŌĆö Fuzzification

Each node is an adaptive node with a node function (Gaussian membership):

$$O_{1,i} = \mu_{A_i}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{x - c_i}{\sigma_i}\right)^{2}\right)$$

where $\{c_i, \sigma_i\}$ are the **premise parameters** to be tuned.

### Layer 2 ŌĆö Rule Antecedent (Firing Strength)

Each node multiplies the incoming membership degrees (T-norm / product):

$$O_{2,i} = w_i = \mu_{A_i}(x) \cdot \mu_{B_i}(y), \quad i = 1, 2$$

### Layer 3 ŌĆö Normalization

$$O_{3,i} = \bar{w}_i = \frac{w_i}{w_1 + w_2}, \quad i = 1, 2$$

### Layer 4 ŌĆö Consequent (Defuzzification Nodes)

Each node is adaptive with node function (first-order TSK polynomial):

$$O_{4,i} = \bar{w}_i \, f_i = \bar{w}_i \, (p_i x + q_i y + r_i)$$

where $\{p_i, q_i, r_i\}$ are the **consequent parameters**.

### Layer 5 ŌĆö Summation (Overall Output)

$$O_{5,1} = f = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \bar{w}_i \, f_i = \sum_{i=1}^{2} w_i f_i \Big/ \sum_{i=1}^{2} w_i$$

### Hybrid Learning Rule

ANFIS uses a **two-pass learning algorithm**:

- **Forward pass**: premise parameters $\{c_i, \sigma_i\}$ are fixed; consequent parameters $\{p_i, q_i, r_i\}$ are estimated by **least-squares** (linear in the consequents).
- **Backward pass**: consequent parameters are fixed; the error is back-propagated to update premise parameters by **gradient descent**.

This hybrid scheme is far faster than pure gradient descent and is the cornerstone of ANFIS optimization.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Single-Neuron Gradient Update

**Problem:** A neuron has weights $\mathbf{w} = (0.3,\, -0.2)^T$ and bias $b = 0.1$. It receives input $\mathbf{x} = (1, 0)^T$ with desired output $d = 1$. Use sigmoid activation, learning rate $\eta = 0.5$, and MSE loss. Compute one gradient-descent update.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Forward pass (net input):**

$$u = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (0.3)(1) + (-0.2)(0) + 0.1 = 0.4$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply sigmoid activation:**

$$y = \sigma(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.4}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{1 + 0.6703} = \frac{1}{1.6703} \approx 0.5987$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the error (MSE half):**

$$E = \frac{1}{2}(d - y)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0.5987)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.4013)^2 \approx 0.0805$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the local gradient at output:**

For a single neuron with sigmoid activation in the output layer and MSE loss, $\delta = (d - y) \cdot \sigma'(u) = (d - y) \cdot y(1 - y)$:

$$\delta = (1 - 0.5987) \cdot 0.5987 \cdot (1 - 0.5987)$$

$$= 0.4013 \cdot 0.5987 \cdot 0.4013 \approx 0.0964$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Update weights and bias:**

$$\Delta w_1 = \eta \, \delta \, x_1 = 0.5 \times 0.0964 \times 1 = 0.0482$$

$$\Delta w_2 = \eta \, \delta \, x_2 = 0.5 \times 0.0964 \times 0 = 0$$

$$\Delta b = \eta \, \delta = 0.5 \times 0.0964 = 0.0482$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö New weights and bias:**

$$w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.3 + 0.0482 = 0.3482$$

$$w_2^{\text{new}} = -0.2 + 0 = -0.2000$$

$$b^{\text{new}} = 0.1 + 0.0482 = 0.1482$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that $w_2$ did **not** change because $x_2 = 0$. This illustrates the fundamental "credit-assignment" challenge in neural networks: weights connected to inactive inputs do not receive gradient signal ŌĆö a key motivation for techniques like **ReLU** and **batch normalization**.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Fuzzy System | Neural Network | Neuro-Fuzzy (ANFIS) |
|--------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Knowledge source | Expert rules | Data | Both |
| Interpretability | **High** | **Low** (black box) | **MediumŌĆōHigh** |
| Learning ability | **None** (rules fixed) | **Strong** | **Strong** |
| Tuning method | Manual / search | Gradient descent | Hybrid (LS + GD) |
| Uncertainty handling | Explicit (MF) | Implicit | Explicit (MF) |
| Optimization parameter set | MF params, rules | Weights, biases | Premise + consequent |
| Universal approximation | Yes (with sufficient rules) | Yes (with sufficient hidden units) | Yes |

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 ŌĆö Biological Neuron vs. Artificial Neuron (Comparative Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIO["BIOLOGICAL NEURON"]
        D1["Dendrites"] --> S1["Soma - Cell Body"]
        S1 --> A1["Axon Hillock"]
        A1 --> AX1["Axon"]
        AX1 --> SY1["Synaptic Terminals"]
    end
    subgraph ART["ARTIFICIAL NEURON"]
        X1["Inputs x1 x2 ... xn"] --> W1["Synaptic Weights w1 w2 ... wn"]

        S2 --> ACT["Activation Function phi"]
        ACT --> Y1["Output y"]
    end
    BIO -. "Modelled as" .-> ART
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping**: Dendrites ŌåÆ Inputs $x_i$ | Synapse ŌåÆ Weight $w_i$ | Soma ŌåÆ Summation + Activation | Axon ŌåÆ Output signal.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 ŌĆö Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) Feedforward Architecture

```mermaid

```

**Layer-by-layer mapping:**

- **Input layer**: 4 nodes $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$
- **Hidden layer 1**: 3 neurons $h_1, h_2, h_3$ with weights $\mathbf{W}^{(1)} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 4}$
- **Hidden layer 2**: 2 neurons $z_1, z_2$ with weights $\mathbf{W}^{(2)} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 3}$
- **Output layer**: 2 outputs $y_1, y_2$ with weights $\mathbf{W}^{(3)} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$

## 4.3 Diagram 3 ŌĆö ANFIS Five-Layer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1 - FUZZIFICATION
    Adaptive nodes
    mu_Ai x = exp of minus x minus ci squared over 2 sigma i squared"]
    L2["LAYER 2 - RULE FIRING
    T norm product
    wi = mu_Ai x times mu_Bi y"]
    L3["LAYER 3 - NORMALIZATION
    Fixed nodes
    w bar i = wi divided by sum of wj"]
    L4["LAYER 4 - CONSEQUENT
    Adaptive linear TSK
    fi = pi x plus qi y plus ri
    node output = w bar i times fi"]
    L5["LAYER 5 - SUMMATION
    Fixed node
    f overall = sum of w bar i times fi"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L5X["OUTPUT f"] -.final.-> L5
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 ŌĆö Training / Optimization Loop (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Initialize weights W and biases b randomly"] --> B["Forward pass compute predicted output y hat"]
    B --> C["Compute loss E of W using y hat and desired d"]
    C --> D{"E less than tolerance or max epochs reached"}
    D -- No --> E["Backward pass compute gradient dE over dW"]
    E --> F["Update W using W new = W old minus eta dE over dW"]
    F --> B
    D -- Yes --> G["Converged final weights W star and model"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization viewpoint** ŌĆö This loop is exactly the **iterative gradient-descent algorithm** you studied in Module 1/2 of PEAET524. The neural network provides the function-evaluation and gradient-computation machinery; the optimization algorithm (GD, Adam, LevenbergŌĆōMarquardt) is the *outer loop*.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 ŌĆö Back-Propagation Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Input x"] --> H["Hidden layer z = phi W x plus b"]
    H --> Y["Output y hat = psi V z plus c"]
    Y --> E["Loss E = half sum d minus y hat squared"]
    E -- delta_L = dE over dY back prop --> Y
    Y -- dE over dV --> V["Update V"]
    Y -- dE over dz chain rule --> H
    H -- delta_l = phi prime times sum delta_k w --> H
    H -- dE over dW --> W["Update W"]
```

## 4.6 Diagram 6 ŌĆö Fuzzy System Optimization Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Initialize MF params ci sigma i and rule base"] --> Q["Fuzzify inputs compute mu values"]
    Q --> R["Apply fuzzy rule base compute firing strengths"]
    R --> S["Defuzzify using centroid or weighted average"]
    S --> T["Compute error E between output and desired target"]
    T --> U{"E less than tolerance"}
    U -- No --> V["Adjust MF params using gradient descent or GA or PSO"]
    V --> Q
    U -- Yes --> W["Optimal fuzzy system ready for deployment"]
```

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define an artificial neuron. With a neat block diagram, explain its mathematical model and list any two activation functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **artificial neuron** is a mathematical abstraction of a biological neuron that processes inputs through weighted summation followed by a non-linear activation. The mathematical model is:

$$y_k = \varphi\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ki} x_i + b_k\right)$$

where $x_i$ are inputs, $w_{ki}$ are weights, $b_k$ is bias, $\varphi$ is the activation function, and $y_k$ is the output. The block diagram consists of: **inputs ŌåÆ multipliers (weights) ŌåÆ summation junction ŌåÆ activation function ŌåÆ output**.

Two activation functions:

1. **Sigmoid**: $\varphi(u) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-u}}$, range $(0,1)$, differentiable everywhere.
2. **ReLU**: $\varphi(u) = \max(0, u)$, range $[0,\infty)$, mitigates vanishing gradient.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**What is ANFIS? State its architecture and the two phases of its hybrid learning algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ANFIS (Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System)** is a hybrid intelligent system proposed by **Jang (1993)** that combines a **TakagiŌĆōSugenoŌĆōKang (TSK) fuzzy inference system** with a **5-layer feedforward neural network**, allowing the fuzzy system parameters to be learned from data.

**Architecture** ŌĆö 5 layers:

1. **Layer 1 (Fuzzification)** ŌĆö adaptive nodes with Gaussian MFs
2. **Layer 2 (Rule firing)** ŌĆö T-norm product
3. **Layer 3 (Normalization)** ŌĆö fixed ratio
4. **Layer 4 (Consequent)** ŌĆö adaptive linear TSK polynomials
5. **Layer 5 (Summation)** ŌĆö fixed overall output

**Two phases of hybrid learning:**

- **Forward pass**: premise parameters fixed ŌåÆ consequent parameters estimated by **least-squares estimator**.
- **Backward pass**: consequent parameters fixed ŌåÆ premise parameters updated by **gradient descent** (back-propagation of error).

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the back-propagation algorithm for a multi-layer feedforward neural network. State clearly the chain of gradient computations. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a 2-2-1 MLP with sigmoid activation at the hidden layer and linear output, perform one forward and backward pass for the input $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1)^T$ and target $d = 0.8$, starting from random weights. Use learning rate $\eta = 0.1$. Show all steps. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) ŌĆö Derivation of Back-Propagation (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Network notation.** Consider an MLP with layers $\ell = 1, 2, \dots, L$. For layer $\ell$:

$$u_j^{(\ell)} = \sum_{i} w_{ji}^{(\ell)} y_i^{(\ell-1)} + b_j^{(\ell)}, \qquad y_j^{(\ell)} = \varphi^{(\ell)}\big(u_j^{(\ell)}\big)$$

with $y_i^{(0)} = x_i$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Loss function.** MSE loss for one sample:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{n_L} \big(d_k - y_k^{(L)}\big)^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Local gradient at output layer.** For neuron $k$ in layer $L$:

$$\delta_k^{(L)} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_k^{(L)}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k^{(L)}} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k^{(L)}}{\partial u_k^{(L)}} = -(d_k - y_k^{(L)}) \cdot \varphi'^{(L)}\big(u_k^{(L)}\big)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Back-propagation to hidden layer.** For hidden neuron $j$ in layer $\ell < L$:

$$\delta_j^{(\ell)} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n_{\ell+1}} \frac{\partial E}{\partial u_k^{(\ell+1)}} \cdot \frac{\partial u_k^{(\ell+1)}}{\partial u_j^{(\ell)}}$$

$$= \sum_{k} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} \cdot w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \cdot \varphi'^{(\ell)}\big(u_j^{(\ell)}\big)$$

$$\boxed{\; \delta_j^{(\ell)} = \varphi'^{(\ell)}\big(u_j^{(\ell)}\big) \sum_{k} \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)} \;}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Weight gradient.** For $w_{ji}^{(\ell)}$:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ji}^{(\ell)}} = \delta_j^{(\ell)} \cdot y_i^{(\ell-1)}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Update rule (gradient descent):**

$$w_{ji}^{(\ell)} \leftarrow w_{ji}^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(\ell)} \, y_i^{(\ell-1)}$$

$$b_j^{(\ell)} \leftarrow b_j^{(\ell)} - \eta \, \delta_j^{(\ell)}$$

This completes the derivation. **Key insight**: the back-propagation algorithm is essentially the **chain rule of calculus applied recursively through the layers of the network**, with the *local gradient* $\delta_j^{(\ell)}$ serving as the carrier of the error signal.

#### Model Solution (b) ŌĆö Numerical Worked Example (7 marks)

**Initial setup.** Let hidden layer have 2 neurons, output layer has 1 neuron. Initialize:

$$\mathbf{W}^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.3 & 0.8 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{(1)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0.2 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{W}^{(2)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.6 & 0.7 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b}^{(2)} = 0.05$$

Input $\mathbf{x} = (1, 1)^T$, target $d = 0.8$, $\eta = 0.1$.

**Forward pass.**

**Hidden layer net input:**

$$u_1^{(1)} = 0.5(1) + 0.4(1) + 0.1 = 1.0$$

$$u_2^{(1)} = 0.3(1) + 0.8(1) + 0.2 = 1.3$$

**Hidden layer output (sigmoid):**

$$y_1^{(1)} = \sigma(1.0) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.0}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.3679} = 0.7311$$

$$y_2^{(1)} = \sigma(1.3) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.3}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.2725} = 0.7858$$

**Output layer net input:**

$$u_1^{(2)} = 0.6(0.7311) + 0.7(0.7858) + 0.05 = 0.4387 + 0.5501 + 0.05 = 1.0388$$

**Output (linear):**

$$y_1^{(2)} = u_1^{(2)} = 1.0388$$

**Loss:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2}(0.8 - 1.0388)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(-0.2388)^2 = 0.0285$$

**Backward pass.**

**Output layer local gradient (linear activation, so $\varphi' = 1$):**

$$\delta_1^{(2)} = (d - y_1^{(2)}) \cdot 1 = 0.8 - 1.0388 = -0.2388$$

**Hidden layer local gradient (sigmoid derivative $\sigma' = \sigma(1-\sigma)$):**

$$\varphi'(u_1^{(1)}) = 0.7311 \times (1 - 0.7311) = 0.7311 \times 0.2689 = 0.1966$$

$$\varphi'(u_2^{(1)}) = 0.7858 \times (1 - 0.7858) = 0.7858 \times 0.2142 = 0.1683$$

$$\delta_1^{(1)} = 0.1966 \times (-0.2388) \times 0.6 = -0.0282$$

$$\delta_2^{(1)} = 0.1683 \times (-0.2388) \times 0.7 = -0.0281$$

**Weight updates.**

**Output layer:**

$$w_{11}^{(2),\text{new}} = 0.6 - 0.1 \times (-0.2388) \times 0.7311 = 0.6 + 0.01745 = 0.6175$$

$$w_{12}^{(2),\text{new}} = 0.7 - 0.1 \times (-0.2388) \times 0.7858 = 0.7 + 0.01876 = 0.7188$$

$$b_1^{(2),\text{new}} = 0.05 - 0.1 \times (-0.2388) = 0.05 + 0.02388 = 0.0739$$

**Hidden layer:**

$$w_{11}^{(1),\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.1 \times (-0.0282) \times 1 = 0.5 + 0.00282 = 0.5028$$

$$w_{12}^{(1),\text{new}} = 0.4 - 0.1 \times (-0.0282) \times 1 = 0.4 + 0.00282 = 0.4028$$

$$w_{21}^{(1),\text{new}} = 0.3 - 0.1 \times (-0.0281) \times 1 = 0.3 + 0.00281 = 0.3028$$

$$w_{22}^{(1),\text{new}} = 0.8 - 0.1 \times (-0.0281) \times 1 = 0.8 + 0.00281 = 0.8028$$

$$b_1^{(1),\text{new}} = 0.1 - 0.1 \times (-0.0282) = 0.1 + 0.00282 = 0.1028$$

$$b_2^{(1),\text{new}} = 0.2 - 0.1 \times (-0.0281) = 0.2 + 0.00281 = 0.2028$$

**Final loss after update** can be re-computed by another forward pass; the loss is expected to drop.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (b)** ŌĆö Students often confuse the **sign of the gradient** and end up *adding* the gradient term instead of *subtracting* it. Remember: gradient descent takes a step in the **negative gradient direction**, hence the **minus sign** in $w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla E$. Also, many forget the **derivative of the activation function** at hidden units ŌĆö without $\varphi'$, the chain rule is incomplete and the update is mathematically wrong. **Up to 3 marks are lost** for these two errors alone.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the optimization of fuzzy systems. Explain the role of membership function parameters and rule-base optimization. List two methods for tuning MF parameters. (7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the ANFIS architecture. For a two-input first-order TSK fuzzy system with two rules, derive the expressions for the output of each of the five layers. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) ŌĆö Optimization of Fuzzy Systems (7 marks)

**Definition.** Optimization of fuzzy systems refers to the systematic adjustment of the parameters governing the behaviour of a fuzzy inference system (FIS) ŌĆö namely the **membership function (MF) parameters**, the **rule weights**, and (less commonly) the **rule structure** itself ŌĆö to minimize a defined objective function such as the prediction error, control cost, or classification loss.

**Role of MF parameters.** Membership functions quantify the *degree of belonging* of an input to a fuzzy set. For a Gaussian MF:

$$\mu_{A_i}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x - c_i}{\sigma_i}\right)^{2}\right)$$

the parameters are the **centre** $c_i$ (location) and the **spread** $\sigma_i$ (width). These determine *where* and *how broadly* a fuzzy region is defined. Mis-tuned MFs lead to:

- **Too narrow MFs** ŌåÆ insufficient overlap, poor rule activation, brittle behaviour.
- **Too wide MFs** ŌåÆ loss of discrimination, sluggish response.
- **Misplaced centres** ŌåÆ wrong linguistic region activated for a given input.

**Role of rule-base optimization.** The rule base defines the *IFŌĆōTHEN* mapping from fuzzy inputs to fuzzy (or crisp) outputs. Optimization can:

- **Tune rule weights** $w_k$ (confidence in each rule).
- **Prune redundant rules**.
- **Discover rules** from data using clustering (e.g., fuzzy c-means) or genetic algorithms.

**Two methods for tuning MF parameters:**

1. **Gradient-based optimization (Back-propagation through the fuzzy system).** Treat $c_i, \sigma_i$ as tunable parameters; compute $\partial E/\partial c_i$ and $\partial E/\partial \sigma_i$ via the chain rule; update by gradient descent. This is exactly the mechanism used in **ANFIS**.

2. **Population-based metaheuristics (Genetic Algorithm, Particle Swarm Optimization, Differential Evolution).** Encode MF parameters as a chromosome / particle; evaluate fitness using the model error; evolve the population. Robust to local minima and gradient discontinuities; works for non-differentiable loss surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> Other methods: **Simulated Annealing**, **Ant Colony Optimization**, **LevenbergŌĆōMarquardt**, **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** ŌĆö all of which are part of the wider PEAET524 toolkit.

#### Model Solution (b) ŌĆö ANFIS Architecture & Layer Derivations (7 marks)

**Block diagram** ŌĆö see Diagram 3 in Section 4 (5-layer adaptive network).

**Setup.** Two inputs $x, y$, one output $f$, two rules:

$$R_1: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_1 \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B_1 \text{ THEN } f_1 = p_1 x + q_1 y + r_1$$

$$R_2: \text{IF } x \text{ is } A_2 \text{ AND } y \text{ is } B_2 \text{ THEN } f_2 = p_2 x + q_2 y + r_2$$

**Layer 1 ŌĆö Fuzzification.** Each node $i$ outputs a Gaussian membership:

$$O_{1,1} = \mu_{A_1}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{x - c_{1}}{\sigma_{1}}\right)^{2}\right)$$

$$O_{1,2} = \mu_{B_1}(y) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{y - c_{2}}{\sigma_{2}}\right)^{2}\right)$$

$$O_{1,3} = \mu_{A_2}(x) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{x - c_{3}}{\sigma_{3}}\right)^{2}\right)$$

$$O_{1,4} = \mu_{B_2}(y) = \exp\!\left(-\left(\frac{y - c_{4}}{\sigma_{4}}\right)^{2}\right)$$

Parameters $\{c_i, \sigma_i\}$ are **premise parameters** (adaptive).

**Layer 2 ŌĆö Rule firing (T-norm product).**

$$O_{2,1} = w_1 = \mu_{A_1}(x) \cdot \mu_{B_1}(y)$$

$$O_{2,2} = w_2 = \mu_{A_2}(x) \cdot \mu_{B_2}(y)$$

**Layer 3 ŌĆö Normalization.**

$$O_{3,1} = \bar{w}_1 = \frac{w_1}{w_1 + w_2}$$

$$O_{3,2} = \bar{w}_2 = \frac{w_2}{w_1 + w_2}$$

**Layer 4 ŌĆö Consequent (adaptive).**

$$O_{4,1} = \bar{w}_1 \cdot f_1 = \bar{w}_1 \, (p_1 x + q_1 y + r_1)$$

$$O_{4,2} = \bar{w}_2 \cdot f_2 = \bar{w}_2 \, (p_2 x + q_2 y + r_2)$$

Parameters $\{p_i, q_i, r_i\}$ are **consequent parameters** (adaptive).

**Layer 5 ŌĆö Summation.**

$$O_{5,1} = f = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \bar{w}_i f_i = \frac{w_1 f_1 + w_2 f_2}{w_1 + w_2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (b)** ŌĆö Students frequently mix up the **inputs to each rule** in Layer 1 (e.g., pairing $\mu_{A_1}$ with $y$ instead of $x$) or **omit the normalization step** (Layer 3), producing a model that is no longer a valid TSK fuzzy system. Also, students forget that in ANFIS the **consequent parameters $\{p_i, q_i, r_i\}$ are linear** ŌĆö this linearity is precisely what allows the **least-squares estimator** to be used in the forward pass. A non-linear consequent would break the hybrid learning rule.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Pin this on your wall before the exam.**

- Ō£ģ An **artificial neuron** computes $y = \varphi(\sum w_i x_i + b)$ ŌĆö weights, bias, and activation are the three pillars.
- Ō£ģ **Activation functions** must be differentiable for back-prop: sigmoid $\sigma(u) = 1/(1+e^{-u})$, tanh, ReLU.
- Ō£ģ The **sigmoid derivative** $\sigma'(u) = \sigma(u)(1 - \sigma(u))$ is the workhorse of classical back-prop.
- Ō£ģ **Perceptron rule** (single layer, step activation): $w^{\text{new}} = w^{\text{old}} + \eta(d - y) x$. Converges only for linearly separable data.
- Ō£ģ **Back-propagation** = chain rule applied recursively. Output $\delta_k^{(L)} = (d_k - y_k) \varphi'(u_k^{(L)})$; hidden $\delta_j^{(\ell)} = \varphi'(u_j^{(\ell)}) \sum_k \delta_k^{(\ell+1)} w_{kj}^{(\ell+1)}$.
- Ō£ģ **Weight update**: $w \leftarrow w - \eta \, \delta \, y_{\text{prev}}$ (always a *minus* sign ŌĆö gradient *descent*).
- Ō£ģ **Loss functions**: MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification. Both are convex in special cases.
- Ō£ģ **Optimization methods** for NN training: GD, SGD, Momentum, AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam, LevenbergŌĆōMarquardt.
- Ō£ģ **Fuzzy system optimization** tunes $\{c_i, \sigma_i\}$ (MF params), $\{p_i, q_i, r_i\}$ (TSK consequent), and rule weights.
- Ō£ģ **ANFIS** = 5-layer adaptive network implementing TSK fuzzy system. Layer 1: fuzzification; Layer 2: rule firing; Layer 3: normalization; Layer 4: consequent; Layer 5: summation.
- Ō£ģ **ANFIS hybrid learning**: forward pass ŌåÆ least squares for consequents; backward pass ŌåÆ gradient descent for premises.
- Ō£ģ **Universal approximation theorem** (Cybenko 1989): an MLP with one hidden layer and a sigmoid activation can approximate any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary accuracy ŌĆö given enough hidden units.
- Ō£ģ **Biological vs. artificial mapping**: dendrites ŌåÆ inputs, synapse ŌåÆ weight, soma ŌåÆ summation + activation, axon ŌåÆ output.
- Ō£ģ **Universal approximator**: BOTH fuzzy systems (with enough rules) and neural networks (with enough hidden units) are universal approximators.
- Ō£ģ **Neuro-fuzzy** advantages: data-driven learning (neural) + linguistic interpretability (fuzzy) + uncertainty handling.
- Ō£ģ **Defuzzification** (Mamdani): $y^* = \frac{\sum_i \mu_i \bar{y}_i}{\sum_i \mu_i}$ ŌĆö centroid of the aggregated MF.
- Ō£ģ **TSK consequent** is linear in inputs: $f_i = p_i x + q_i y + r_i$ ŌĆö different from the constant consequent of a zero-order TSK or the fuzzy set of Mamdani.
- Ō£ģ **KTU mark-distribution tip**: A typical 14-mark question has 2 sub-parts ├Ś 7 marks each. Spend 1ŌĆō2 minutes planning; write the formula, *then* explain the formula; numerical sub-parts must show **all** intermediate calculations.
- Ō£ģ **Examiner's golden rule**: If the question says "derive", every intermediate algebraic step must be visible. If it says "explain", a neat block diagram + 4ŌĆō5 bullet points suffices. If it says "compute", *show all numerical substitutions*.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 notes on Optimization of Fuzzy Systems and Basics of Neural Network Based Optimization.** For Module 5 (typically Genetic Algorithms / Simulated Annealing), the same gradient-descent-vs-metaheuristic dichotomy continues ŌĆö keep your ANFIS derivation and back-prop derivation as templates; they will resurface in modified form.
